The **Dominican Republic**[[a]](#cite_note-16) is a country on the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") island of [Hispaniola](/wiki/Hispaniola "Hispaniola") in the [Greater Antilles](/wiki/Greater_Antilles "Greater Antilles") of the [Caribbean Sea](/wiki/Caribbean_Sea "Caribbean Sea") in the [North Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"). It shares a [maritime border](/wiki/Maritime_boundary "Maritime boundary") with [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico") to the east and [a land border](/wiki/Dominican_Republic%E2%80%93Haiti_border "Dominican Republic–Haiti border") with [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti") to the west, occupying the [eastern five-eighths](/wiki/Geography_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Geography of the Dominican Republic") of Hispaniola which, along with [Saint Martin](/wiki/Saint_Martin_(island) "Saint Martin (island)"), is one of only two islands in the Caribbean shared by two [sovereign states](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state").[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18) In the [Antilles](/wiki/Antilles "Antilles"), the country is the [second-largest](/wiki/List_of_Caribbean_islands_by_area "List of Caribbean islands by area") nation by area after [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba") at 48,671 square kilometers (18,792 sq mi) and [second-largest](/wiki/List_of_Caribbean_countries_by_population "List of Caribbean countries by population") by population after Haiti with approximately 11.4 million people in 2024, of whom 3.6 million reside in [the metropolitan area](/wiki/Greater_Santo_Domingo "Greater Santo Domingo") of [Santo Domingo](/wiki/Santo_Domingo "Santo Domingo"), the capital city.[[4]](#cite_note-CIADemo-4)

The native [Taíno](/wiki/Ta%C3%ADno "Taíno") people had inhabited Hispaniola prior to [European contact](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas"), dividing it into [five chiefdoms](/wiki/Chiefdoms_of_Hispaniola "Chiefdoms of Hispaniola"). [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") claimed the island for [Castile](/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile"), landing there on his [first voyage in 1492](/wiki/Voyages_of_Christopher_Columbus#First_voyage "Voyages of Christopher Columbus"). The [colony of Santo Domingo](/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_Santo_Domingo "Captaincy General of Santo Domingo") became the site of the first permanent European settlement in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"). In 1697, Spain recognized French dominion over the western third of the island, which became the independent [First Empire of Haiti](/wiki/First_Empire_of_Haiti "First Empire of Haiti") in 1804. A group of Dominicans deposed the Spanish governor and declared independence from Spain in November 1821, but were annexed by Haiti in February 1822. Independence came 22 years later in 1844, after victory in the [Dominican War of Independence](/wiki/Dominican_War_of_Independence "Dominican War of Independence"). The next 72 years saw several civil wars, failed invasions by Haiti, and a brief return to Spanish colonial status, before permanently ousting the Spanish during the [Dominican Restoration War](/wiki/Dominican_Restoration_War "Dominican Restoration War") of 1863–1865. From 1930, the dictatorship of [Rafael Trujillo](/wiki/Rafael_Trujillo "Rafael Trujillo") ruled until his assassination in 1961. [Juan Bosch](/wiki/Juan_Bosch_(politician) "Juan Bosch (politician)") was elected president in 1962 but was deposed in a [military coup in 1963](/wiki/1963_Dominican_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1963 Dominican coup d'état"). The [Dominican Civil War of 1965](/wiki/Dominican_Civil_War "Dominican Civil War") preceded the authoritarian rule of [Joaquín Balaguer](/wiki/Joaqu%C3%ADn_Balaguer "Joaquín Balaguer") (1966–1978 and 1986–1996). Since 1996, the Dominican Republic has moved towards [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy").

The Dominican Republic has the [largest economy](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Economy of the Dominican Republic") in the Caribbean and the [seventh-largest](/wiki/List_of_Latin_American_and_Caribbean_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of Latin American and Caribbean countries by GDP (PPP)") in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America").[[18]](#cite_note-gdp_rank-19)[[19]](#cite_note-worldbank.org-20) Over the last 25 years, the Dominican Republic has had the fastest-growing economy in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere") – with an average [real GDP growth rate](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_real_GDP_growth_rate "List of countries by real GDP growth rate") of 5.3% between 1992 and 2018.[[20]](#cite_note-:0-21) GDP growth in 2014 and 2015 reached 7.3 and 7.0%, respectively, the highest in the Western Hemisphere.[[20]](#cite_note-:0-21) Recent growth has been driven by construction, manufacturing, tourism, and mining. The country is the site of the world's third largest [gold mine](/wiki/Gold_mine "Gold mine") in terms of production, the [Pueblo Viejo mine](/wiki/Pueblo_Viejo_mine "Pueblo Viejo mine").[[21]](#cite_note-investingnews.com-22)[[22]](#cite_note-lawrieongold.com-23) The gold production of the country was 31 metric tonnes in 2015.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

The Dominican Republic is the [most visited destination](/wiki/World_Tourism_rankings "World Tourism rankings") in the Caribbean.[[24]](#cite_note-WTO_Tourism_Highlights_2018_Edition-25) A geographically diverse nation, the Dominican Republic is home to both the Caribbean's tallest mountain peak, [Pico Duarte](/wiki/Pico_Duarte "Pico Duarte"), and the Caribbean's largest lake and lowest point, [Lake Enriquillo](/wiki/Lake_Enriquillo "Lake Enriquillo").[[25]](#cite_note-Baker_2008_190-26) The island has an average temperature of 26 °C (78.8 °F) and great climatic and biological diversity.[[26]](#cite_note-consulate-27) The country is also the site of the first [cathedral](/wiki/Cathedral_of_Santo_Domingo "Cathedral of Santo Domingo"), [palace](/wiki/Alc%C3%A1zar_de_Col%C3%B3n "Alcázar de Colón"), [monastery](/wiki/Monastery_of_San_Francisco,_Santo_Domingo "Monastery of San Francisco, Santo Domingo"), and [fortress](/wiki/Fortaleza_Ozama "Fortaleza Ozama") built in the Americas, located in Santo Domingo's [Colonial Zone](/wiki/Ciudad_Colonial_(Santo_Domingo) "Ciudad Colonial (Santo Domingo)"), a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site").[[27]](#cite_note-Colonial_City_of_Santo_Domingo-28)[[28]](#cite_note-unesco.org-29)

## Etymology

The name Dominican originates from [Saint Dominic](/wiki/Saint_Dominic "Saint Dominic"),[[29]](#cite_note-30) the patron saint of [astronomers](/wiki/Astronomer "Astronomer"), and founder of the [Dominican Order](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order"). The Dominican Order established what is now known as the [Universidad Autónoma de Santo Domingo](/wiki/Universidad_Santo_Tom%C3%A1s_de_Aquino "Universidad Santo Tomás de Aquino"), the first university in the New World.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

For most of its history, up until independence, the colony was known simply as **Santo Domingo**[[31]](#cite_note-firstcolony-32) and continued to be commonly known as such in English until the early 20th century.[[32]](#cite_note-33) The residents were called "Dominicans" (*Dominicanos*), the adjectival form of "Domingo", and as such, the revolutionaries named their newly independent country the "Dominican Republic" (*la República Dominicana*).

In the [national anthem of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/National_Anthem_of_the_Dominican_Republic "National Anthem of the Dominican Republic") (*himno nacional de la República Dominicana*), the poetic term "Quisqueyans" (*Quisqueyanos*) is used instead of "Dominicans". The word "Quisqueya" derives from the [Ciguayo language](/wiki/Ciguayo_language "Ciguayo language"), and means "mother of the lands". It is often used in songs as another name for the country. The name of the country in English is often shortened to "the D.R." (*la R.D.*), but this is rare in Spanish.[[33]](#cite_note-Kraft-34)

## History

Main article: [History of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/History_of_the_Dominican_Republic "History of the Dominican Republic")

### Pre-Columbian era

Main article: [Chiefdoms of Hispaniola](/wiki/Chiefdoms_of_Hispaniola "Chiefdoms of Hispaniola")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Copia_de_Cacicazgos_de_la_Hispaniola.png/250px-Copia_de_Cacicazgos_de_la_Hispaniola.png)](/wiki/File:Copia_de_Cacicazgos_de_la_Hispaniola.png)

The five [caciquedoms](/wiki/Cacicazgo "Cacicazgo") of Hispaniola (c. 1492).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Pictografia_igneri.jpg/120px-Pictografia_igneri.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pictografia_igneri.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Ta%C3%ADno_pictographs_Cuevas_de_las_Maravillas.jpg/250px-Ta%C3%ADno_pictographs_Cuevas_de_las_Maravillas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ta%C3%ADno_pictographs_Cuevas_de_las_Maravillas.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/LosTresOjos.JPG/330px-LosTresOjos.JPG)](/wiki/File:LosTresOjos.JPG)

The [Pomier Caves](/wiki/Pomier_Caves "Pomier Caves") are a series of 55 caves located north of San Cristóbal. They contain the largest collection of 2,000-year-old rock art in the Caribbean.

The islands of the Caribbean were first settled around 6,000 years ago by hunter-gatherer peoples originating from Central America or northern South America. The [Arawakan-speaking](/wiki/Arawakan_languages "Arawakan languages") ancestors of the [Taíno](/wiki/Ta%C3%ADno "Taíno") moved into the Caribbean from South America during the 1st millennium BCE, reaching Hispaniola by around 600 CE.[[34]](#cite_note-:13-35) These Arawakan peoples engaged in farming, fishing,[[35]](#cite_note-Encarta-36) hunting and gathering,[[36]](#cite_note-LunaC-37) and the widespread production of [ceramic](/wiki/Ceramic "Ceramic") goods.[[34]](#cite_note-:13-35)

The estimates of Hispaniola's population in 1492 vary widely, ranging from tens of thousands[[37]](#cite_note-38) to 2 million.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

By 1492, the island was divided into five Taíno chiefdoms.[[39]](#cite_note-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41) The Taíno name for the entire island was either *Ayiti* or *Quisqueya*.[[41]](#cite_note-anghiera-42)

### European colonization

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Bas%C3%ADlica_Menor_de_Santa_Mar%C3%ADa_SD_RD_02_2017_1947.jpg/250px-Bas%C3%ADlica_Menor_de_Santa_Mar%C3%ADa_SD_RD_02_2017_1947.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bas%C3%ADlica_Menor_de_Santa_Mar%C3%ADa_SD_RD_02_2017_1947.jpg)

[Catedral Santa María La Menor](/wiki/Catedral_Santa_Mar%C3%ADa_La_Menor "Catedral Santa María La Menor") is the oldest cathedral built in the Americas

[Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") arrived on the island on December 5, 1492, during the first of his [four voyages](/wiki/Voyages_of_Christopher_Columbus "Voyages of Christopher Columbus") to the Americas. He claimed the land for Spain and named it *La Española*, due to its diverse climate and terrain, which reminded him of the [Spanish](/wiki/Spain "Spain") landscape.[[42]](#cite_note-43) In 1496, [Bartholomew Columbus](/wiki/Bartholomew_Columbus "Bartholomew Columbus"), Christopher's brother, built the city of [Santo Domingo](/wiki/Ciudad_Colonial_(Santo_Domingo) "Ciudad Colonial (Santo Domingo)"), Western Europe's first permanent settlement in the "[New World](/wiki/New_World "New World")". The Spaniards created a [plantation economy](/wiki/Plantation_economy "Plantation economy").[[43]](#cite_note-transat-44)

Initially, after friendly relationships, the Taínos resisted the conquest, led by female Chief [Anacaona](/wiki/Anacaona "Anacaona") of Xaragua and her ex-husband Chief [Caonabo](/wiki/Caonabo "Caonabo") of Maguana, as well as Chiefs [Guacanagaríx](/wiki/Guacanagar%C3%ADx "Guacanagaríx"), [Guamá](/wiki/Guam%C3%A1 "Guamá"), [Hatuey](/wiki/Hatuey "Hatuey"), and [Enriquillo](/wiki/Enriquillo "Enriquillo"). The latter's successes gained his people an autonomous enclave on the island. Within a few years after 1492, the population of Taínos had declined drastically due to [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox"),[[44]](#cite_note-45) measles, and other diseases that arrived with the Europeans.[[45]](#cite_note-history-46) African slaves were imported to replace the dwindling Taínos.

The last record of pure Taínos in the country was from 1864. Still, Taíno biological heritage survived due to intermixing. Census records from 1514 reveal that 40% of Spanish men in Santo Domingo were married to Taíno women,[[46]](#cite_note-47) and some present-day Dominicans have Taíno ancestry.[[47]](#cite_note-Guitar-48)[[48]](#cite_note-Martinez-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Treaty_of_Aranjuez_English.svg/250px-Treaty_of_Aranjuez_English.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Treaty_of_Aranjuez_English.svg)

Map showing the border situation on Hispaniola following the [Treaty of Aranjuez (1777)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Aranjuez_(1777) "Treaty of Aranjuez (1777)")

By the time of the [Treaty of Ryswick](/wiki/Treaty_of_Ryswick "Treaty of Ryswick") in 1697, which ceded the western one-third of the island to France, the population of Santo Domingo consisted of a few thousand whites, approximately 30,000 black slaves, and a few Taínos.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003341-50) By 1789, the population had grown to 125,000, but Santo Domingo remained one of Spain's less wealthy and strategically important colonies in the New World.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003341-50) The population composition of Santo Domingo sharply contrasted with that of the neighboring French colony of [Saint-Domingue](/wiki/Saint-Domingue "Saint-Domingue")—the wealthiest colony in the Caribbean and whose population of half a million was 90% enslaved and four times as numerous as Santo Domingo.[[50]](#cite_note-google48-51)[[31]](#cite_note-firstcolony-32)

In 1795, Spain ceded Santo Domingo to France by the [Treaty of Basel](/wiki/Peace_of_Basel "Peace of Basel") as a result of its defeat in the [War of the Pyrenees](/wiki/War_of_the_Pyrenees "War of the Pyrenees"). Saint-Domingue achieved independence as Haiti from France on January 1, 1804. In 1809, the French were expelled from the island and Santo Domingo returned to [Spanish rule](/wiki/Espa%C3%B1a_Boba "España Boba").[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003342-52)

### Ephemeral independence and Haitian occupation

Main articles: [Republic of Spanish Haiti](/wiki/Republic_of_Spanish_Haiti "Republic of Spanish Haiti") and [Haitian occupation of Santo Domingo](/wiki/Haitian_occupation_of_Santo_Domingo "Haitian occupation of Santo Domingo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/LaTrinitaria.jpg/250px-LaTrinitaria.jpg)](/wiki/File:LaTrinitaria.jpg)

In 1838, [Juan Pablo Duarte](/wiki/Juan_Pablo_Duarte "Juan Pablo Duarte") and others established a secret patriotic society called *[La Trinitaria](/wiki/La_Trinitaria_(Dominican_Republic) "La Trinitaria (Dominican Republic)")*, which helped undermine Haitian occupation.

After a dozen years of discontent and failed independence plots by various opposing groups, Santo Domingo's former Lieutenant-Governor (top administrator), [José Núñez de Cáceres](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_N%C3%BA%C3%B1ez_de_C%C3%A1ceres "José Núñez de Cáceres"), declared the colony's independence from the [Spanish crown](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") as [Spanish Haiti](/wiki/Republic_of_Spanish_Haiti "Republic of Spanish Haiti"), on November 30, 1821. This period is also known as the Ephemeral independence.[[52]](#cite_note-Bethell1986-53)

The newly independent republic ended two months later, when it was occupied and annexed by Haiti, then under the leadership of [Jean-Pierre Boyer](/wiki/Jean-Pierre_Boyer "Jean-Pierre Boyer").[[53]](#cite_note-guitar-54) For twenty-two years, Haiti controlled Santo Domingo, which it called *Partie de l'Est*, treating it as a colonial territory. The unpaid Haitian army sustained itself by taking resources from the Dominican people and land without compensation.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003343-55)

### First Republic (1844–1861)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Flag_of_the_Dominican_Republic_%281844%29.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_Dominican_Republic_%281844%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_Dominican_Republic_(1844).svg)

Original [flag of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Flag_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Flag of the Dominican Republic") (up to 1849)

In 1838, [Juan Pablo Duarte](/wiki/Juan_Pablo_Duarte "Juan Pablo Duarte") founded a secret society called [La Trinitaria](/wiki/La_Trinitaria_(Dominican_Republic) "La Trinitaria (Dominican Republic)"), which sought the complete independence of Santo Domingo without any foreign intervention.[[55]](#cite_note-pons-56): p147–149 Also [Francisco del Rosario Sánchez](/wiki/Francisco_del_Rosario_S%C3%A1nchez "Francisco del Rosario Sánchez") and [Ramon Matias Mella](/wiki/Ramon_Matias_Mella "Ramon Matias Mella"), despite not being among the founding members of La Trinitaria, were decisive in the fight for independence. Duarte, Mella, and Sánchez are considered the Founding Fathers of the Dominican Republic.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

On February 27, 1844, the members of *La Trinitaria* declared independence from Haiti. They were backed by [Pedro Santana](/wiki/Pedro_Santana "Pedro Santana"), a wealthy cattle rancher, who became general of the army of the nascent republic. The decades that followed were filled with tyranny, factionalism, economic difficulties, rapid changes of government, and exile for political opponents. Archrivals Santana and [Buenaventura Báez](/wiki/Buenaventura_B%C3%A1ez "Buenaventura Báez") held power most of the time, both ruling arbitrarily. They promoted competing plans to annex the new nation to a major power. The Dominican Republic's first [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Constitution of the Dominican Republic") was adopted on November 6, 1844, and its population in 1845 was approximately 230,000 people (100,000 whites; 40,000 blacks; and 90,000 mulattoes).[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003343-55)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Santana.gif/250px-Santana.gif)](/wiki/File:Santana.gif)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Buenaventura_Ba%C3%A9z.gif/250px-Buenaventura_Ba%C3%A9z.gif)](/wiki/File:Buenaventura_Ba%C3%A9z.gif)

[Pedro Santana](/wiki/Pedro_Santana "Pedro Santana") and [Buenaventura Báez](/wiki/Buenaventura_B%C3%A1ez "Buenaventura Báez"), the *[caudillos](/wiki/Caudillo "Caudillo")* who led the Dominican Republic during its first republican period

In March 1844, Haiti invaded but the Dominicans put up stiff opposition and inflicted heavy casualties on the Haitians.[[57]](#cite_note-Bethell-58) By April 15, Dominican forces had defeated the Haitian forces on both land and sea.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003344-59) In early July 1844, Duarte was urged by his followers to take the title of President of the Republic. Duarte agreed, but only if free elections were arranged. However, Santana's forces took Santo Domingo on July 12, and they declared Santana ruler of the Dominican Republic. Santana then put Mella, Duarte, and Sánchez in jail. On February 27, 1845, Santana executed [María Trinidad Sánchez](/wiki/Mar%C3%ADa_Trinidad_S%C3%A1nchez "María Trinidad Sánchez"), heroine of La Trinitaria, and others for conspiracy.

After defeating a third Haitian invasion in April 1849 at the [Battle of Las Carreras](/wiki/Battle_of_Las_Carreras "Battle of Las Carreras"), Santana marched on Santo Domingo and deposed president [Manuel Jimenes](/wiki/Manuel_Jimenes "Manuel Jimenes") (who had ousted Santana as president) in a [coup d'état](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Coup d'état"). At his behest, Congress elected Buenaventura Báez as president, but Báez was unwilling to serve as Santana's puppet. In November–December 1849, Dominican seamen raided the Haitian coasts, plundered seaside villages, as far as [Dame Marie](/wiki/Dame-Marie,_Grand%27Anse "Dame-Marie, Grand'Anse"), and butchered crews of captured enemy ships.[[59]](#cite_note-60)[[60]](#cite_note-61) A fourth and final invasion by Haiti in November 1855 was defeated by Dominican forces by January 27, 1856, resulting in thousands of Haitian casualties.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003347-62) Again Santana and Báez plotted against each other for political dominance, with Báez winning the first encounter and expelling Santana in 1857, and Santana winning the second and expelling Báez in 1859.

### Restoration republic

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Monumento_a_los_H%C3%A9roes_de_la_Restauraci%C3%B3n_2013.jpg/250px-Monumento_a_los_H%C3%A9roes_de_la_Restauraci%C3%B3n_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Monumento_a_los_H%C3%A9roes_de_la_Restauraci%C3%B3n_2013.jpg)

Monument built by Dominicans in 1944, commemorating the participants of the War of Restoration.

In 1861, after imprisoning, exiling, and executing many of his opponents and due to political and economic reasons, Santana asked Queen [Isabella II of Spain](/wiki/Isabella_II_of_Spain "Isabella II of Spain") to retake control of the Dominican Republic. Spain, which had not come to terms with the loss of its mainland American colonies 40 years earlier, [annexed the country](/wiki/Annexation_of_the_Dominican_Republic_to_Spain "Annexation of the Dominican Republic to Spain").[[62]](#cite_note-63) The island was occupied by 30,000 Spanish troops bolstered by battalions of Cuban and Puerto Rican volunteers and 12,000 Dominicans who aligned themselves with the Spanish forces.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003347-62) The Haitian rebel [Sylvain Salnave](/wiki/Sylvain_Salnave "Sylvain Salnave"), fearful of the reestablishment of Spain as colonial power, gave refuge and logistics to revolutionaries seeking to reestablish the independent nation. The ensuing civil war, known as the *War of Restoration*, killed more than 50,000.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

The war began on August 16, 1863; by 1865, the Dominican forces had confined the Spanish troops to Santo Domingo, and the Spaniards were afraid to venture outside the capital.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003349-65) After nearly two years of fighting, Spain abandoned the island in July 1865. One military historian estimates Spanish casualties at 10,888 killed or wounded in action and thousands dead from [yellow fever](/wiki/Yellow_fever "Yellow fever"), while the Dominican forces fighting for Spain suffered 10,000 casualties.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClodfelter2017306-66) Another military historian estimates that Spain lost 18,000 dead, a figure that does not include the Dominicans, Cubans, and Puerto Ricans fighting alongside them.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003349-65) The Dominicans fighting for independence against Spain suffered more than 4,000 dead.

Political strife again prevailed in the following years; warlords ruled, military revolts were extremely common, and the nation amassed debt. It was now Báez's turn to act on his [plan of annexing the country to the United States](/wiki/Proposed_annexation_of_Santo_Domingo "Proposed annexation of Santo Domingo"), where two successive presidents were supportive. U.S. President [Ulysses S. Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant") desired a naval base at [Samaná](/wiki/Saman%C3%A1_Bay "Samaná Bay") and also a place for resettling newly freed African Americans.[[66]](#cite_note-67) The treaty was defeated in the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") in 1870.[[53]](#cite_note-guitar-54)[[67]](#cite_note-68)[[68]](#cite_note-69)[[69]](#cite_note-70) Báez was toppled in 1874, returned, and was toppled for good in 1878.

Relative peace came to the country in the 1880s, which saw the coming to power of General [Ulises Heureaux](/wiki/Ulises_Heureaux "Ulises Heureaux").[[70]](#cite_note-countrystudies-71) "Lilís", as the new president was nicknamed, put the nation deep into debt while using much of the proceeds for his personal use and to maintain his police state.[[70]](#cite_note-countrystudies-71)[[71]](#cite_note-72) In 1899, he was assassinated. However, the relative calm over which he presided allowed improvement in the Dominican economy. The sugar industry was modernized,[[72]](#cite_note-Hall-73): p10 and the country attracted foreign workers and immigrants. Lebanese, Syrians, and Palestinians from the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") began to arrive in the country during the latter part of the 19th century.[[73]](#cite_note-Brown-74) They were referred to locally as "Turks" because they were Ottoman subjects and carried Ottoman passports. During the U.S. occupation of 1916–24, Dominican peasants would not only kill U.S. Marines, but would also attack and kill Arab vendors traveling through the countryside.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

### 20th century (1900–1930)

From 1902 on, short-lived governments were again the norm, with their power usurped by caudillos in parts of the country. Furthermore, the national government was bankrupt and, unable to pay its debts to European creditors, faced the threat of military intervention by France, [Germany](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire"), and [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy").[[75]](#cite_note-cs9-76) United States President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") sought to prevent European intervention, largely to protect the routes to the future [Panama Canal](/wiki/Panama_Canal "Panama Canal"). He made a small military intervention to ward off European powers, to proclaim his famous [Roosevelt Corollary](/wiki/Roosevelt_Corollary "Roosevelt Corollary") to the [Monroe Doctrine](/wiki/Monroe_Doctrine "Monroe Doctrine"), and also to obtain his 1905 Dominican agreement for U.S. administration of Dominican customs, which was the chief source of income for the Dominican government. A 1906 agreement provided for the arrangement to last 50 years. The United States agreed to use part of the customs proceeds to reduce the immense foreign debt of the Dominican Republic and assumed responsibility for said debt.[[35]](#cite_note-Encarta-36)[[75]](#cite_note-cs9-76)

After six years in power, President [Ramón Cáceres](/wiki/Ram%C3%B3n_C%C3%A1ceres "Ramón Cáceres") (who had himself assassinated Heureaux)[[70]](#cite_note-countrystudies-71) was assassinated in 1911. The result was several years of great political instability and [civil war](/wiki/Dominican_Civil_War_(1911%E2%80%931912) "Dominican Civil War (1911–1912)"). U.S. mediation by the [William Howard Taft](/wiki/William_Howard_Taft "William Howard Taft") and [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") administrations achieved only a short respite each time. A political deadlock in 1914 was broken after an ultimatum by Wilson telling the Dominicans to choose a president or see the U.S. impose one. A provisional president was chosen, and later the same year relatively free elections put former president (1899–1902) [Juan Isidro Jimenes Pereyra](/wiki/Juan_Isidro_Jimenes_Pereyra "Juan Isidro Jimenes Pereyra") back in power. With his former [Secretary of War](/wiki/Secretary_of_War "Secretary of War") [Desiderio Arias](/wiki/Desiderio_Arias "Desiderio Arias") maneuvering to depose him and despite a U.S. offer of military aid against Arias, Jimenes resigned on May 7, 1916.[[76]](#cite_note-congress-77) Wilson thus ordered the U.S. occupation of the Dominican Republic.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/USMC_Fortaleza_Ozama_from_river_1922_restored.jpg/250px-USMC_Fortaleza_Ozama_from_river_1922_restored.jpg)](/wiki/File:USMC_Fortaleza_Ozama_from_river_1922_restored.jpg)

The flag of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") waving over [Ozama Fortress](/wiki/Fortaleza_Ozama "Fortaleza Ozama") during the U.S. occupation of the Dominican Republic, c. 1922

U.S. Marines landed on May 16, 1916, and seized the capital and other ports, while General Arias fell back to his inland Santiago stronghold.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003b49-78) [Fort San Felipe](/wiki/Fortaleza_San_Felipe "Fortaleza San Felipe") was captured on June 1 by U.S. Marines after a battle against Dominicans.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMusicant1990249-79) A significant weaponry disparity led to Arias's defeat as his forces tried to block the Marines' advance on Santiago with trenches and black-powder rifles.[[79]](#cite_note-80) The clashes with the U.S. Marines marked the first time that the Dominicans had ever encountered a machine gun.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMusicant1990252-81) A peace delegation from Santiago surrendered the city on July 5, coinciding with General Arias' surrender to the Dominican governor. The military government established by the U.S. under the Navy and Marine Corps on November 29, led by Vice Admiral [Harry Shepard Knapp](/wiki/Harry_Shepard_Knapp "Harry Shepard Knapp"), was widely repudiated by the Dominicans, but organized resistance ceased.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003b52-82)

The occupation regime kept most Dominican laws and institutions and largely [pacified](/wiki/Pacification_(military_action) "Pacification (military action)") the general population. The occupying government also revived the Dominican economy, reduced the nation's debt, built a road network that at last interconnected all regions of the country, and created a professional National Guard to replace the warring partisan units.[[76]](#cite_note-congress-77) Additionally, with grass-roots support from local communities and assistance from both Dominican and US officials, the Dominican education system expanded significantly during US occupation. Between 1918 and 1920, more than three hundred schools were established nationwide.[[82]](#cite_note-83) The system of forced labour used by the [Marines in Haiti](/wiki/United_States_occupation_of_Haiti "United States occupation of Haiti") was largely absent in the Dominican Republic.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses197817-84)

The U.S. government's rule ended in October 1922, and elections were held in March 1924.[[76]](#cite_note-congress-77) The victor was former president (1902–03) [Horacio Vásquez](/wiki/Horacio_V%C3%A1squez "Horacio Vásquez"). He was inaugurated on July 13, 1924, and the last U.S. forces left in September.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMusicant1990284-85) In 1930, General [Rafael Trujillo](/wiki/Rafael_Trujillo "Rafael Trujillo"), who was trained by the U.S. Marines during the occupation,[[85]](#cite_note-nyt-86) seized power following a military revolt against the government of Vásquez.

Trujillo consolidated his power after [Hurricane San Zenón](/wiki/Hurricane_San_Zen%C3%B3n "Hurricane San Zenón") devastated Santo Domingo in September 1930, killing 8,000 people. A few of the former caudillos initially opposed the new dictator. General Cipriano Bencosme led an uprising but was defeated and killed in November 1930 during a confrontation with the army near Puerto Plata. General Desiderio Arias was also unsuccessful, dying in combat near [Mao](/wiki/Mao,_Dominican_Republic "Mao, Dominican Republic") in June of the following year.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses197822-87)

### Trujillo Era (1930–1961)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Rafael_L._Trujillo_en_la_silla_presidencial_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Rafael_L._Trujillo_en_la_silla_presidencial_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rafael_L._Trujillo_en_la_silla_presidencial_(cropped).jpg)

[Rafael Trujillo](/wiki/Rafael_Trujillo "Rafael Trujillo") imposed a dictatorship of 31 years (1930–1961).

There was considerable economic growth during [Rafael Trujillo](/wiki/Rafael_Trujillo "Rafael Trujillo")'s long and iron-fisted regime, although a great deal of the wealth was taken by the dictator and other regime elements. There was progress in healthcare, education, and transportation, with the building of hospitals, clinics, schools, roads, and harbors. Trujillo also carried out an important housing construction program and instituted a pension plan. He finally negotiated an undisputed border with Haiti in 1935, and achieved the end of the 50-year customs agreement in 1941, well before it would have expired in 1956. He made the country debt-free in 1947.[[35]](#cite_note-Encarta-36)[[87]](#cite_note-Galvan-88) This was accompanied most noticeably by absolute repression and the copious use of murder, torture, and terrorist methods against the opposition.

Starting in 1935, several Dominicans were assassinated in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") after taking part in anti-Trujillo activities.[[85]](#cite_note-nyt-86) In October 1937, under Trujillo's orders, Dominican troops killed tens of thousands of Haitian men, women, and children on the Haitian-Dominican border.[[85]](#cite_note-nyt-86)

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Trujillo symbolically sided with the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II"). During the [Battle of the Caribbean](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Caribbean "Battle of the Caribbean") in 1942, German U-boats torpedoed and sank two Dominican-flagged merchant vessels—the *San Rafael* off the coast of Jamaica and the *[Presidente Trujillo](/wiki/SS_Presidente_Trujillo "SS Presidente Trujillo")* off [Fort-de-France](/wiki/Fort-de-France "Fort-de-France")—along with four other Dominican-manned ships in the Caribbean. The country did not contribute militarily, but its sugar and other agricultural products supported the Allied war effort.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Participantes_de_la_invasi%C3%B3n_de_Trinidad_en_Cuba_arrestados.jpg/250px-Participantes_de_la_invasi%C3%B3n_de_Trinidad_en_Cuba_arrestados.jpg)](/wiki/File:Participantes_de_la_invasi%C3%B3n_de_Trinidad_en_Cuba_arrestados.jpg)

Captured members of Trujillo's [Anti-Communist Legion](/wiki/Anti-Communist_Legion_of_the_Caribbean "Anti-Communist Legion of the Caribbean") in Trinidad, Cuba, August 13, 1959.

Under Trujillo, a weapons factory at [San Cristóbal](/wiki/San_Crist%C3%B3bal_Province "San Cristóbal Province") produced rifles, machine guns, and ammunition.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003b175-89) Trujillo also formed a right-wing Foreign Legion of 3,000 mercenaries to attempt to overthrow [Fidel Castro](/wiki/Fidel_Castro "Fidel Castro") in Cuba. Major [William Morgan](/wiki/William_Alexander_Morgan "William Alexander Morgan"), acting as a double agent for Castro, pretended to lead the operation for Trujillo but secretly informed Castro of the plot. On August 13, 1959, a [C-47](/wiki/Douglas_C-47_Skytrain "Douglas C-47 Skytrain") transport flying from the Dominican Republic carrying military advisors and supplies landed at [Trinidad](/wiki/Trinidad,_Cuba "Trinidad, Cuba") airport, where Castro seized the aircraft and its ten occupants; an exchange of gunfire left two of the advisors and three Cuban forces dead, and around 4,000 suspects were arrested throughout Cuba.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003b240-90) Earlier in June, Cuban soldiers and Cuban-trained Dominican guerrillas attempted an [unsuccessful invasion](/wiki/Foreign_interventions_by_Cuba#1959_Dominican_Republic_invasion_attempt "Foreign interventions by Cuba") of the Dominican Republic.[[90]](#cite_note-91)

On November 25, 1960, Trujillo's henchmen killed three of the four [Mirabal sisters](/wiki/Mirabal_sisters "Mirabal sisters"), nicknamed *Las Mariposas* (The Butterflies). Along with their husbands, the sisters were conspiring to overthrow Trujillo in a violent revolt. The [International Day for the Elimination of Violence against Women](/wiki/International_Day_for_the_Elimination_of_Violence_against_Women "International Day for the Elimination of Violence against Women") is observed on the anniversary of their deaths.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/1960._Junio%2C_24._Atentado_a_R%C3%B3mulo_Betancourt_en_Los_Pr%C3%B3ceres.jpg/250px-1960._Junio%2C_24._Atentado_a_R%C3%B3mulo_Betancourt_en_Los_Pr%C3%B3ceres.jpg)](/wiki/File:1960._Junio,_24._Atentado_a_R%C3%B3mulo_Betancourt_en_Los_Pr%C3%B3ceres.jpg)

Aftermath of the June 24, 1960, assassination attempt.

For a long time, the U.S. and the Dominican elite supported the Trujillo government. This support persisted despite the assassinations of political opposition, the [massacre of Haitians](/wiki/Parsley_massacre "Parsley massacre"), and Trujillo's plots against other countries.[[91]](#cite_note-Wucker-92) The U.S. finally broke with Trujillo in 1960, after Trujillo's agents attempted to assassinate the Venezuelan president [Rómulo Betancourt](/wiki/R%C3%B3mulo_Betancourt "Rómulo Betancourt") with a car bomb, as he was a fierce critic of Trujillo.[[92]](#cite_note-locdr11-93)[[93]](#cite_note-94) The assassination attempt in [Caracas](/wiki/Caracas "Caracas") wounded the Venezuelan president and his Minister of Defense, and killed an air force colonel and a policeman. During Betancourt's earlier exile in Cuba, Trujillo's agents attempted to inject poison into him on a [Havana](/wiki/Havana "Havana") street in broad daylight.[[94]](#cite_note-Sifakis-95)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/KMO_157030_03580_1_t236_204404.jpg/250px-KMO_157030_03580_1_t236_204404.jpg)](/wiki/File:KMO_157030_03580_1_t236_204404.jpg)

Trujillo's car following the May 30, 1961, assassination.

After its representatives confirmed Trujillo's complicity in the nearly successful assassination attempt, the [Organization of American States](/wiki/Organization_of_American_States "Organization of American States") (OAS), for the first time in its history, decreed sanctions against a member state.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses197828-96) The OAS voted unanimously to condemn the Dominican Republic for its aggression and imposed an arms embargo.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003b256-97) The United States severed diplomatic relations with the Dominican Republic on August 26, 1960, and in January 1961 suspended the export of trucks, parts, crude oil, gasoline and other petroleum products. U.S. President [Dwight D. Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Dwight D. Eisenhower") also took advantage of OAS sanctions to drastically cut purchases of Dominican sugar, the country's major export. This action ultimately cost the Dominican Republic almost $22,000,000 in lost revenues at a time when its economy was in a rapid decline. Trujillo had become expendable, and dissidents inside the Dominican Republic argued that assassination was the only certain way to remove him.[[97]](#cite_note-Cord_Meyer-98)[[98]](#cite_note-CIA-99)

On May 30, 1961, Trujillo was shot and killed by Dominican dissidents during a car chase.[[85]](#cite_note-nyt-86)

On May 31, 1961, the following day, Venezuela arrested several individuals plotting to overthrow the government, armed with weapons traced to the Dominican Republic.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003b257-100)

### Post-Trujillo (1961–1996)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Juan_Bosch_%281963%29.jpg/250px-Juan_Bosch_%281963%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Juan_Bosch_(1963).jpg)

[Juan Bosch](/wiki/Juan_Bosch_(politician) "Juan Bosch (politician)"), the first democratically elected president after Trujillo

After the assassination, [Ramfis Trujillo](/wiki/Ramfis_Trujillo "Ramfis Trujillo"), the dictator's son, remained in de facto control of the government for the next 6 months, as commander of the armed forces. Trujillo's brothers, Hector Bienvenido and Jose Arismendi Trujillo, returned to the country and plotted against President Balaguer. On November 18, 1961, as a planned coup became more evident, U.S. Secretary of State [Dean Rusk](/wiki/Dean_Rusk "Dean Rusk") issued a warning that the US would not "remain idle" if the Trujillos attempted to "reassert dictatorial domination". Following this warning, and the arrival of a 14-vessel U.S. naval task force within sight of Santo Domingo, Ramfis and his uncles fled the country on November 19. The OAS lifted its sanctions on January 4, 1962.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses197862-101)

In February 1963, a democratically elected government under leftist [Juan Bosch](/wiki/Juan_Bosch_(politician) "Juan Bosch (politician)") took office but it was overthrown by a military coup in September. On April 24, 1965, a second military coup ousted the military-installed president [Donald Reid Cabral](/wiki/Donald_Reid_Cabral "Donald Reid Cabral").[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMusicant1990363-102) Despite tank assaults, [strafing](/wiki/Strafing "Strafing"), aerial bombardment, and [rocket](/wiki/High_Velocity_Aircraft_Rocket "High Velocity Aircraft Rocket") attacks by junta forces, pro-Bosch Constitutionalists maintained control of most of the capital, where electricity, water, and food supplies collapsed amid the fighting.[[102]](#cite_note-time-103) By April 26, around 5,000 armed civilians had joined the 1,500 rebel military regulars. The total strength of the junta was eight [F-51](/wiki/North_American_P-51_Mustang "North American P-51 Mustang") fighters, eight [Vampire](/wiki/De_Havilland_Vampire "De Havilland Vampire") jets, a company of 23 tanks, and two infantry battalions totaling 1,700 troops.[[102]](#cite_note-time-103)

On April 28, U.S. President [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") deployed U.S. Marines from [Guantanamo Bay Naval Base](/wiki/Guantanamo_Bay_Naval_Base "Guantanamo Bay Naval Base") to Santo Domingo to protect American citizens and support the [military junta](/wiki/Military_junta "Military junta") that had ousted Bosch in the 1963 coup, with U.S. forces subsequently expanded to 24,000 troops.[[85]](#cite_note-nyt-86) On April 30, elements of the [82nd Airborne Division](/wiki/82nd_Airborne_Division "82nd Airborne Division") landed from [C‑124](/wiki/Douglas_C-124_Globemaster_II "Douglas C-124 Globemaster II") and [C‑130](/wiki/Lockheed_C-130_Hercules "Lockheed C-130 Hercules") transports at [San Isidro airfield](/wiki/San_Isidro_Air_Base "San Isidro Air Base") and crossed the Duarte Bridge under the cover of [F-4 Phantom](/wiki/McDonnell_Douglas_F-4_Phantom_II "McDonnell Douglas F-4 Phantom II") jets to link up with junta troops, who were to secure a corridor for the Marines guarding the U.S. Embassy. However, the junta troops withdrew to San Isidro airfield instead.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003b276-104) On May 2, U.S. forces were authorized to link up, and the outgunned Constitutionalists retreated to the southeastern part of the city. A ceasefire was declared on May 5. The following day, U.S. diplomats persuaded the OAS to establish an [Inter-American Peace Force](/wiki/Inter-American_Peace_Force "Inter-American Peace Force") to support American troops. The following countries volunteered: Brazil (1,250 soldiers), Costa Rica (25 police), Honduras (250 soldiers), Nicaragua (164 soldiers), and Paraguay (286 soldiers).[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScheina2003b277-105)

U.S. and OAS peacekeeping troops remained in the country for over a year and left after supervising elections in 1966 won by [Joaquín Balaguer](/wiki/Joaqu%C3%ADn_Balaguer "Joaquín Balaguer"). He had been Trujillo's last puppet-president.[[35]](#cite_note-Encarta-36)[[105]](#cite_note-civil_war-106) Balaguer remained in power as president for 12 years. His tenure was a period of repression of human rights and civil liberties.[[106]](#cite_note-govinfo-107) His rule was criticized for a growing disparity between rich and poor. It was, however, praised for an ambitious infrastructure program, which included construction of large housing projects, sports complexes, theaters, museums, aqueducts, roads, highways, and the massive [Columbus Lighthouse](/wiki/Columbus_Lighthouse "Columbus Lighthouse"), completed in 1992 during a later tenure.

In 1978, Balaguer was succeeded to the presidency by opposition candidate [Antonio Guzmán Fernández](/wiki/Antonio_Guzm%C3%A1n_Fern%C3%A1ndez "Antonio Guzmán Fernández"), of the [Dominican Revolutionary Party](/wiki/Dominican_Revolutionary_Party "Dominican Revolutionary Party") (PRD). [Hurricane David](/wiki/Hurricane_David "Hurricane David") hit the Dominican Republic in August 1979, which left upwards of 2,000 people dead and 200,000 homeless. The hurricane caused over $1 billion in damage. Another PRD win in 1982 followed, under [Salvador Jorge Blanco](/wiki/Salvador_Jorge_Blanco "Salvador Jorge Blanco"). Balaguer regained the presidency in 1986 and was re-elected in 1990 and 1994, in the latter defeating PRD candidate [José Francisco Peña Gómez](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Francisco_Pe%C3%B1a_G%C3%B3mez "José Francisco Peña Gómez"), a former mayor of Santo Domingo. The 1994 elections were flawed, bringing international pressure, to which Balaguer responded by scheduling another presidential contest in 1996. Balaguer was not a candidate. The PSRC candidate was his Vice President [Jacinto Peynado Garrigosa](/wiki/Jacinto_Peynado_Garrigosa "Jacinto Peynado Garrigosa").[[107]](#cite_note-108)

### 1996–present

In 1996, with the support of Joaquín Balaguer and the Social Christian Reform Party in a coalition called the Patriotic Front, [Leonel Fernández](/wiki/Leonel_Fern%C3%A1ndez "Leonel Fernández") achieved the first-ever win for the [Dominican Liberation Party](/wiki/Dominican_Liberation_Party "Dominican Liberation Party") (PLD),[[108]](#cite_note-109) which Bosch had founded in 1973 after leaving the PRD. Fernández oversaw a fast-growing economy: growth averaged 7.7% per year, unemployment fell, and exchange and inflation rates were stable.[[109]](#cite_note-coha-110)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Protestas_dominicanas_en_Santo_Domingo_2020.jpg/250px-Protestas_dominicanas_en_Santo_Domingo_2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Protestas_dominicanas_en_Santo_Domingo_2020.jpg)

[2020 Dominican Republic protests](/wiki/2020_Dominican_Republic_protests "2020 Dominican Republic protests") in Plaza de La Bandera, Santo Domingo

In 2000, the PRD's [Hipólito Mejía](/wiki/Hip%C3%B3lito_Mej%C3%ADa "Hipólito Mejía") won the election. This was a time of economic troubles.[[109]](#cite_note-coha-110) Under Mejía, the Dominican Republic participated in the US-led coalition, as part of the Multinational [Plus Ultra Brigade](/wiki/Plus_Ultra_Brigade "Plus Ultra Brigade"), during the 2003 [invasion of Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"), suffering no casualties. In 2008, Fernández was elected for a third term.[[110]](#cite_note-ussdnote-111) Fernández and the PLD are credited with initiatives that moved the country forward technologically. His administrations were accused of corruption.[[111]](#cite_note-112)[[109]](#cite_note-coha-110)

[Danilo Medina](/wiki/Danilo_Medina "Danilo Medina") of the PLD was elected president in 2012 and re-elected in 2016. A significant increase in crime, government corruption and a weak justice system threatened to overshadow their administrative period.[[112]](#cite_note-113)[[113]](#cite_note-114) He was succeeded by the opposition candidate [Luis Abinader](/wiki/Luis_Abinader "Luis Abinader") in the [2020 election](/wiki/2020_Dominican_Republic_general_election "2020 Dominican Republic general election") (weeks after [protests erupted in the country](/wiki/2020_Dominican_Republic_protests "2020 Dominican Republic protests") against Medina's government), marking the end to 16 years in power of the centre-left Dominican Liberation Party (PLD).[[114]](#cite_note-115)[[115]](#cite_note-116) In May 2024, President Luis Abinader won a second term in the [elections](/wiki/2024_Dominican_Republic_general_election "2024 Dominican Republic general election"). His tough policies towards migration from neighbouring Haiti was popular among voters.[[116]](#cite_note-117)

## Geography

Main articles: [Geography of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Geography_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Geography of the Dominican Republic") and [List of islands of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_the_Dominican_Republic "List of islands of the Dominican Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Dominican_Republic_relief_location_map.jpg/500px-Dominican_Republic_relief_location_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dominican_Republic_relief_location_map.jpg)

Topographical map of Dominican Republic

The Dominican Republic comprises the eastern five-eighths of [Hispaniola](/wiki/Hispaniola "Hispaniola"), the second-largest island in the [Greater Antilles](/wiki/Greater_Antilles "Greater Antilles"), with the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") to the north and the [Caribbean Sea](/wiki/Caribbean_Sea "Caribbean Sea") to the south. It shares the island roughly at a 2:1 ratio with [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti"), the north-to-south (though somewhat irregular) border between the two countries being 376 km (234 mi).[[4]](#cite_note-CIADemo-4) To the north and north-west lie [The Bahamas](/wiki/The_Bahamas "The Bahamas") and the [Turks and Caicos Islands](/wiki/Turks_and_Caicos_Islands "Turks and Caicos Islands"), and to the east, across the [Mona Passage](/wiki/Mona_Passage "Mona Passage"), the US Commonwealth of [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"). The country's area is reported variously as 48,442 km2 (18,704 sq mi) (by the embassy in the United States)[[15]](#cite_note-embassy-15) and 48,670 km2 (18,792 sq mi),[[4]](#cite_note-CIADemo-4) making it the second largest country in the [Antilles](/wiki/Antilles "Antilles"), after [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"). The Dominican Republic's capital and largest city [Santo Domingo](/wiki/Santo_Domingo "Santo Domingo") is on the southern coast.[[4]](#cite_note-CIADemo-4) A major fault system, the Septentrional fault system, representing the intersection of the North American plate and the Caribbean plate runs through the Dominican Republic.[[117]](#cite_note-118)[[118]](#cite_note-119)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Monte_Cristi%2C_Coastline.JPG/250px-Monte_Cristi%2C_Coastline.JPG)](/wiki/File:Monte_Cristi,_Coastline.JPG)

[Monte Cristi](/wiki/Monte_Cristi,_Dominican_Republic "Monte Cristi, Dominican Republic") coastline

|first4=Luis R.

The Dominican Republic has four important mountain ranges. The most northerly is the *[Cordillera Septentrional](/wiki/Cordillera_Septentrional "Cordillera Septentrional")* ("Northern Mountain Range"), which extends from the northwestern coastal town of [Monte Cristi](/wiki/San_Fernando_de_Monte_Cristi "San Fernando de Monte Cristi"), near the Haitian border, to the [Samaná Peninsula](/wiki/Saman%C3%A1_Peninsula "Samaná Peninsula") in the east, running parallel to the Atlantic coast. The highest range in the Dominican Republic – indeed, in the whole of the West Indies – is the *[Cordillera Central](/wiki/Cordillera_Central,_Dominican_Republic "Cordillera Central, Dominican Republic")* ("Central Mountain Range"). In the Cordillera Central are the four highest peaks in the Caribbean: [Pico Duarte](/wiki/Pico_Duarte "Pico Duarte") (3,098 metres or 10,164 feet above sea level),[[4]](#cite_note-CIADemo-4) La Pelona (3,094 metres or 10,151 feet), La Rucilla (3,049 metres or 10,003 feet), and Pico Yaque (2,760 metres or 9,055 feet). In the southwest corner of the country, south of the Cordillera Central, there are two other ranges: the more northerly of the two is the *[Sierra de Neiba](/w/index.php?title=Sierra_de_Neiba&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sierra de Neiba (page does not exist)")*, while in the south the *[Sierra de Bahoruco](/wiki/Sierra_de_Bahoruco "Sierra de Bahoruco")* is a continuation of the [Massif de la Selle](/wiki/Massif_de_la_Selle "Massif de la Selle") in Haiti. There are other, minor mountain ranges, such as the *Cordillera Oriental* ("Eastern Mountain Range"), *Sierra Martín García*, *Sierra de Yamasá*, and *Sierra de Samaná*.

Between the Central and Northern mountain ranges lies the rich and fertile [Cibao](/wiki/Cibao "Cibao") valley. This major valley is home to the cities of [Santiago de los Caballeros](/wiki/Santiago_de_los_Caballeros "Santiago de los Caballeros") and [La Vega](/wiki/La_Vega,_Dominican_Republic "La Vega, Dominican Republic") and most of the farming areas of the nation. Rather less productive are the semi-arid San Juan Valley, south of the Central Cordillera, and the Neiba Valley, tucked between the Sierra de Neiba and the Sierra de Bahoruco. Much of the land around the [Enriquillo](/wiki/Lake_Enriquillo "Lake Enriquillo") Basin is below sea level, with a hot, arid, desert-like environment. There are other smaller valleys in the mountains, such as the [Constanza](/wiki/Constanza,_Dominican_Republic "Constanza, Dominican Republic"), [Jarabacoa](/wiki/Jarabacoa "Jarabacoa"), [Villa Altagracia](/wiki/Villa_Altagracia "Villa Altagracia"), and [Bonao](/wiki/Bonao "Bonao") valleys.
The *Llano Costero del Caribe* ("Caribbean Coastal Plain") is the largest of the plains in the Dominican Republic. Stretching north and east of Santo Domingo, it contains many sugar plantations in the [savannahs](/wiki/Savannah "Savannah") that are common there. West of Santo Domingo its width is reduced to 10 kilometres (6.2 mi) as it hugs the coast, finishing at the mouth of the Ocoa River. Another large plain is the *Plena de Azua* ("Azua Plain"), a very arid region in [Azua Province](/wiki/Azua_Province "Azua Province"). A few other small coastal plains are on the northern coast and in the [Pedernales](/wiki/Pedernales_Province "Pedernales Province") Peninsula.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Salto_el_Lim%C3%B3n_%2814688207924%29.jpg/250px-Salto_el_Lim%C3%B3n_%2814688207924%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Salto_el_Lim%C3%B3n_(14688207924).jpg)

Salto del Limón, one of many waterfalls across the Dominican Republic

Four major rivers drain the numerous mountains of the Dominican Republic. The [Yaque del Norte](/wiki/Yaque_del_Norte_River "Yaque del Norte River") is the longest and most important Dominican river. It carries excess water down from the Cibao Valley and empties into Monte Cristi Bay, in the northwest. Likewise, the [Yuna River](/wiki/Yuna_River "Yuna River") serves the Vega Real and empties into Samaná Bay, in the northeast. Drainage of the San Juan Valley is provided by the San Juan River, [tributary](/wiki/Tributary "Tributary") of the [Yaque del Sur](/wiki/Yaque_del_Sur "Yaque del Sur"), which empties into the Caribbean, in the south. The [Artibonito](/wiki/Artibonite_River "Artibonite River") is the longest river of Hispaniola and flows westward into Haiti. There are many lakes and coastal lagoons. The largest lake is [Enriquillo](/wiki/Lake_Enriquillo "Lake Enriquillo"), a [salt lake](/wiki/Salt_lake "Salt lake") at 45 metres (148 ft) below sea level, the lowest elevation in the Caribbean.[[4]](#cite_note-CIADemo-4)

There are many small offshore islands and [cays](/wiki/Cay "Cay") that form part of the Dominican territory. The two largest islands near shore are [Saona](/wiki/Saona_Island "Saona Island"), in the southeast, and [Beata](/wiki/Beata_Island "Beata Island"), in the southwest. Smaller islands include the [Cayos Siete Hermanos](/w/index.php?title=Cayos_Siete_Hermanos&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cayos Siete Hermanos (page does not exist)"), [Isla Cabra](/w/index.php?title=Isla_Cabra&action=edit&redlink=1 "Isla Cabra (page does not exist)"), [Cayo Jackson](/w/index.php?title=Cayo_Jackson&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cayo Jackson (page does not exist)"), [Cayo Limón](/w/index.php?title=Cayo_Lim%C3%B3n&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cayo Limón (page does not exist)"), [Cayo Levantado](/wiki/Cayo_Levantado "Cayo Levantado"), [Cayo la Bocaina](/w/index.php?title=Cayo_la_Bocaina&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cayo la Bocaina (page does not exist)"), [Catalanita](/wiki/Catalina_Island_(Dominican_Republic) "Catalina Island (Dominican Republic)"), [Cayo Pisaje](/w/index.php?title=Cayo_Pisaje&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cayo Pisaje (page does not exist)") and [Isla Alto Velo](/wiki/Isla_Alto_Velo "Isla Alto Velo"). To the north, at distances of 100–200 kilometres (62–124 mi), are three extensive, largely submerged [banks](/wiki/Bank_(topography) "Bank (topography)"), which geographically are a southeast continuation of [the Bahamas](/wiki/The_Bahamas "The Bahamas"): [Navidad Bank](/wiki/Navidad_Bank "Navidad Bank"), [Silver Bank](/wiki/Silver_Bank "Silver Bank"), and [Mouchoir Bank](/wiki/Mouchoir_Bank "Mouchoir Bank"). Navidad Bank and Silver Bank have been officially claimed by the Dominican Republic.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Isla Cabritos](/w/index.php?title=Isla_Cabritos&action=edit&redlink=1 "Isla Cabritos (page does not exist)") lies within [Lago Enriquillo](/wiki/Lago_Enriquillo "Lago Enriquillo").

The country is home to five terrestrial ecoregions: [Hispaniolan moist forests](/wiki/Hispaniolan_moist_forests "Hispaniolan moist forests"), [Hispaniolan dry forests](/wiki/Hispaniolan_dry_forests "Hispaniolan dry forests"), [Hispaniolan pine forests](/wiki/Hispaniolan_pine_forests "Hispaniolan pine forests"), [Enriquillo wetlands](/wiki/Enriquillo_wetlands "Enriquillo wetlands"), and [Greater Antilles mangroves](/wiki/Greater_Antilles_mangroves "Greater Antilles mangroves").[[119]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-120)

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Climate_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Climate of the Dominican Republic")

The Dominican Republic has a [tropical rainforest climate](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest_climate "Tropical rainforest climate")[[120]](#cite_note-Izzo_Rosskopf_New_Climatic_Map-121) in the coastal and lowland areas. Some areas, such as most of the [Cibao](/wiki/Cibao "Cibao") region, have a [tropical savanna climate](/wiki/Tropical_savanna_climate "Tropical savanna climate").[[120]](#cite_note-Izzo_Rosskopf_New_Climatic_Map-121) Due to its diverse topography, Dominican Republic's climate shows considerable variation over short distances and is the most varied of all the Antilles. The annual average temperature is 25 °C (77 °F). At higher elevations the temperature averages 18 °C (64.4 °F) while near sea level the average temperature is 28 °C (82.4 °F). Low temperatures of 0 °C (32 °F) are possible in the mountains while high temperatures of 40 °C (104 °F) are possible in protected valleys. January and February are the coolest months of the year while August is the hottest month. Snowfall can be seen on rare occasions on the summit of [Pico Duarte](/wiki/Pico_Duarte "Pico Duarte").[[121]](#cite_note-DRcli-122)

The [wet season](/wiki/Wet_season "Wet season") along the northern coast lasts from November through January. Elsewhere the wet season stretches from May through November, with May being the wettest month. Average annual rainfall is 1,500 millimetres (59.1 in) countrywide, with individual locations in the Valle de Neiba seeing averages as low as 350 millimetres (13.8 in) while the Cordillera Oriental averages 2,740 millimetres (107.9 in). The driest part of the country lies in the west.[[121]](#cite_note-DRcli-122)

[Tropical cyclones](/wiki/Tropical_cyclones "Tropical cyclones") strike the Dominican Republic every couple of years, with 65% of the impacts along the southern coast. Hurricanes are most likely between June and October.[[121]](#cite_note-DRcli-122)[[4]](#cite_note-CIADemo-4) The last major hurricane that struck the country was [Hurricane Georges](/wiki/Hurricane_Georges "Hurricane Georges") in 1998.[[122]](#cite_note-HURDAT-123)

### Fauna

[Bats](/wiki/Bat "Bat") make up 90% of the native terrestrial mammal species residing in the Dominican Republic.[[123]](#cite_note-124) Lake Enriquillo, located in the Dominican Republic's southwest, is home to the largest population of [American crocodiles](/wiki/American_crocodile "American crocodile").[[124]](#cite_note-125)

## Government and politics

Main article: [Politics of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Politics_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Politics of the Dominican Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Luis_Abinader_%2825-04-2024%29.jpg/250px-Luis_Abinader_%2825-04-2024%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Luis_Abinader_(25-04-2024).jpg)

[Luis Abinader](/wiki/Luis_Abinader "Luis Abinader")  
[President](/wiki/President_of_the_Dominican_Republic "President of the Dominican Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/20250524_Quito_posesion_del_presidente_Daniel_Noboa-Ovidio_Gonz%C3%A1lez2497_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-20250524_Quito_posesion_del_presidente_Daniel_Noboa-Ovidio_Gonz%C3%A1lez2497_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:20250524_Quito_posesion_del_presidente_Daniel_Noboa-Ovidio_Gonz%C3%A1lez2497_(cropped).jpg)

[Raquel Peña](/wiki/Raquel_Pe%C3%B1a_(politician) "Raquel Peña (politician)")  
[Vice President](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Vice President of the Dominican Republic")

The Dominican Republic is a [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") or [democratic republic](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy"),[[15]](#cite_note-embassy-15)[[4]](#cite_note-CIADemo-4)[[110]](#cite_note-ussdnote-111) with three branches of power: executive, [legislative](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature"), and [judicial](/wiki/Judiciary "Judiciary"). The [president of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/President_of_the_Dominican_Republic "President of the Dominican Republic") heads the executive branch and executes laws passed by the [congress](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Congress of the Dominican Republic"), appoints the cabinet, and is commander in chief of the [armed forces](/wiki/Military_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Military of the Dominican Republic"). The president and vice-president run for office on the same ticket and are elected by direct vote for four-year terms. The national legislature is bicameral, composed of a [senate](/wiki/Senate_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Senate of the Dominican Republic"), which has 32 members, and the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Chamber of Deputies of the Dominican Republic"), with 190 members.[[110]](#cite_note-ussdnote-111)

Judicial authority rests with the [Supreme Court of Justice](/wiki/Government_of_the_Dominican_Republic#Judicial_branch "Government of the Dominican Republic")'s 16 members. The court "alone hears actions against the president, designated members of his Cabinet, and members of Congress when the legislature is in session."[[110]](#cite_note-ussdnote-111) The court is appointed by a council known as the [National Council of the Magistracy](/wiki/National_Council_of_the_Magistracy_(Dominican_Republic) "National Council of the Magistracy (Dominican Republic)") which is composed of the president, the leaders of both houses of Congress, the President of the Supreme Court, and an opposition or non–governing-party member.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Santo_Domingo_National_Palace.jpg/250px-Santo_Domingo_National_Palace.jpg)](/wiki/File:Santo_Domingo_National_Palace.jpg)

The [National Palace](/wiki/National_Palace_(Dominican_Republic) "National Palace (Dominican Republic)") in Santo Domingo

The Dominican Republic has a [multi-party political system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system"). Elections are held every two years, alternating between the [presidential elections](/wiki/Elections_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Elections in the Dominican Republic"), which are held in years evenly divisible by four, and the congressional and municipal elections, which are held in even-numbered years not divisible by four. "International observers have found that presidential and congressional elections since 1996 have been generally free and fair."[[110]](#cite_note-ussdnote-111) Starting in 2016, elections are held jointly, after a constitutional reform.[[125]](#cite_note-126)

The three major parties are the conservative [Social Christian Reformist Party](/wiki/Social_Christian_Reformist_Party "Social Christian Reformist Party") ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Partido Reformista Social Cristiano (PRSC)*), in power 1966–78 and 1986–96; and the [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") [Dominican Revolutionary Party](/wiki/Dominican_Revolutionary_Party "Dominican Revolutionary Party") ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Partido Revolucionario Dominicano (PRD)*), in power in 1963, 1978–86, and 2000–04; and the [Dominican Liberation Party](/wiki/Dominican_Liberation_Party "Dominican Liberation Party") ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Partido de la Liberación Dominicana (PLD)*), in power 1996–2000 and 2004–2020. In 2020, [protests erupted](/wiki/2020_Dominican_Republic_protests "2020 Dominican Republic protests") against the PLD's rule. The presidential candidate for the opposition [Modern Revolutionary Party](/wiki/Modern_Revolutionary_Party "Modern Revolutionary Party") (PRM), [Luis Abinader](/wiki/Luis_Abinader "Luis Abinader"), won the [election](/wiki/2020_Dominican_Republic_general_election "2020 Dominican Republic general election"), defeating the PLD.[[126]](#cite_note-127) Abinader was re-elected to a second term as president in the [2024 Dominican Republic general election](/wiki/2024_Dominican_Republic_general_election "2024 Dominican Republic general election"). In his victory speech, Abinader said that the "changes that we've made are going to be irreversible", and that the "best is yet to come".[[127]](#cite_note-apvictory-128)

According to [International IDEA](/wiki/International_Institute_for_Democracy_and_Electoral_Assistance "International Institute for Democracy and Electoral Assistance")’s Global State of Democracy (GSoD) Indices and Democracy Tracker, the Dominican Republic performs in the mid-range on overall democratic measures, with particular strengths in civil society, inclusive suffrage, and free political parties.[[128]](#cite_note-129)

### Administrative divisions

Main articles: [Provinces of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Provinces_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Provinces of the Dominican Republic") and [Municipalities of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Municipalities_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Municipalities of the Dominican Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Dominican_Republic%2C_administrative_divisions_-_de_-_monochrome.svg/250px-Dominican_Republic%2C_administrative_divisions_-_de_-_monochrome.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Dominican_Republic,_administrative_divisions_-_de_-_monochrome.svg)

Provinces of the Dominican Republic

The Dominican Republic is divided into 31 [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Provinces of the Dominican Republic"). Santo Domingo, the capital, is designated [Distrito Nacional](/wiki/Distrito_Nacional "Distrito Nacional") (National District). The provinces are divided into municipalities (*[municipios](/wiki/Municipio "Municipio")*; singular *municipio*). They are the second-level political and [administrative subdivisions](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") of the country. The president appoints the governors of the 31 provinces. Mayors and municipal councils administer the 124 municipal districts and the National District (Santo Domingo). They are elected at the same time as congressional representatives.[[110]](#cite_note-ussdnote-111)

The provinces are the first–level administrative subdivisions of the country. The headquarters of the central government's regional offices are normally found in the capital cities of provinces. The president appoints an administrative governor (*Gobernador Civil*) for each province but not for the Distrito Nacional (Title IX of the constitution).[[129]](#cite_note-Constitution-130)
The Distrito Nacional was created in 1936. Prior to this, the Distrito National was the old Santo Domingo Province, in existence since the country's independence in 1844. It is not to be confused with the new Santo Domingo Province split off from it in 2001. While it is similar to a province in many ways, the Distrito Nacional differs in its lack of an administrative governor and consisting only of one municipality, [Santo Domingo](/wiki/Santo_Domingo "Santo Domingo"), the city council (*[ayuntamiento](/wiki/Ayuntamiento "Ayuntamiento")*) and mayor (*síndico*) which are in charge of its administration.[[130]](#cite_note-131)
