### Foreign relations

Further information: [Foreign relations of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Foreign relations of the Dominican Republic")

The Dominican Republic has a close relationship with the [United States](/wiki/Dominican_Republic-United_States_relations "Dominican Republic-United States relations"), and has close cultural ties with the [Commonwealth of Puerto Rico](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Puerto_Rico "Commonwealth of Puerto Rico"), and other states and jurisdictions of the United States.

The Dominican Republic's [relationship with neighbouring Haiti](/wiki/Dominican_Republic%E2%80%93Haiti_relations "Dominican Republic–Haiti relations") is strained over mass Haitian migration to the Dominican Republic, with citizens of the Dominican Republic blaming the Haitians for increased crime and other social problems.[[131]](#cite_note-132) The Dominican Republic is an observer member of the *[Organisation Internationale de la Francophonie](/wiki/Organisation_Internationale_de_la_Francophonie "Organisation Internationale de la Francophonie")*.[[132]](#cite_note-133)

The Dominican Republic has a [free trade agreement](/wiki/Free-trade_area "Free-trade area") with the United States, Costa Rica, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras and Nicaragua via the [Dominican Republic-Central America Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/Dominican_Republic%E2%80%93Central_America_Free_Trade_Agreement "Dominican Republic–Central America Free Trade Agreement")[[133]](#cite_note-134) and an [economic partnership agreement](/wiki/Economic_Partnership_Agreements "Economic Partnership Agreements") with the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and the [Caribbean Community](/wiki/Caribbean_Community "Caribbean Community") via the [Caribbean Forum.](/wiki/CARIFORUM "CARIFORUM")[[134]](#cite_note-135)

The Dominican Republic ties with [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan") as the 79th most peaceful country in the world, according to the 2025 [Global Peace Index](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index").[[135]](#cite_note-136)

### Military

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Two_Commando_soldiers_of_the_MEFA_provide_security.jpg/250px-Two_Commando_soldiers_of_the_MEFA_provide_security.jpg)](/wiki/File:Two_Commando_soldiers_of_the_MEFA_provide_security.jpg)

Dominican soldiers training in Santo Domingo

Main article: [Armed Forces of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Armed Forces of the Dominican Republic")

The Armed Forces of the Dominican Republic are the military forces of the Dominican Republic. They consist of approximately 56,000 active duty personnel.[[136]](#cite_note-IISS409-137) The President of the Dominican Republic is the [commander in chief](/wiki/Commander_in_chief "Commander in chief") of the Armed Forces of the Dominican Republic and the Ministry of Defense is the chief managing body of the armed forces.

The [Army](/wiki/Dominican_Army "Dominican Army"), with 28,750 active duty personnel,[[136]](#cite_note-IISS409-137) consists of six [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") brigades, an air cavalry squadron and a combat service support brigade. The [Air Force](/wiki/Dominican_Air_Force "Dominican Air Force") operates two main bases, one in the southern region near [Santo Domingo](/wiki/Santo_Domingo "Santo Domingo") and one in the northern region of the country, the air force operates approximately 75 aircraft including helicopters. The [Navy](/wiki/Dominican_Navy "Dominican Navy") operates two major naval bases, one in Santo Domingo and one in Las Calderas on the southwestern coast.

The armed forces have organized a Specialized Airport Security Corps (CESA) and a Specialized Port Security Corps (CESEP) to meet international security needs in these areas. The secretary of the armed forces has also announced plans to form a specialized border corps (CESEF). The armed forces provide 75% of personnel to the National Investigations Directorate (DNI) and the Counter-Drug Directorate (DNCD).[[110]](#cite_note-ussdnote-111)

In 2018, Dominican Republic signed the UN [treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Prohibition_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons").[[137]](#cite_note-138)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Economy of the Dominican Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/SantoDomingoedit.JPG/250px-SantoDomingoedit.JPG)](/wiki/File:SantoDomingoedit.JPG)

View of [Santo Domingo](/wiki/Santo_Domingo "Santo Domingo"), the Dominican Republic's capital city

During the last three decades, the Dominican economy, formerly dependent on the export of agricultural commodities (mainly sugar, cocoa and coffee), has transitioned to a diversified mix of services, manufacturing, agriculture, mining, and trade. The service sector accounts for almost 60% of GDP; manufacturing, for 22%; tourism, telecommunications and finance are the main components of the service sector; however, none of them accounts for more than 10% of the whole.[[138]](#cite_note-139) The Dominican Republic has a stock market, [Bolsa de Valores de la República Dominicana](/wiki/Bolsa_de_Valores_de_la_Rep%C3%BAblica_Dominicana "Bolsa de Valores de la República Dominicana") (BVRD).[[139]](#cite_note-140) and advanced telecommunication system and transportation infrastructure.[[26]](#cite_note-consulate-27) High unemployment and income inequality are long-term challenges.[[4]](#cite_note-CIADemo-4) International migration affects the Dominican Republic greatly, as it receives and sends large flows of migrants. Mass illegal Haitian immigration and the integration of Dominicans of Haitian descent are major issues.[[140]](#cite_note-pinadep-141) A large Dominican diaspora exists, mostly [in the United States](/wiki/Dominican_Americans_(Dominican_Republic) "Dominican Americans (Dominican Republic)"),[[141]](#cite_note-s0201-142) contributes to development, sending billions of dollars to Dominican families in remittances.[[4]](#cite_note-CIADemo-4)[[110]](#cite_note-ussdnote-111)

[Remittances](/wiki/Remittance "Remittance") in Dominican Republic increased to US$4571.30 million in 2014 from US$3333 million in 2013 (according to data reported by the Inter-American Development Bank). Economic growth takes place in spite of a chronic energy shortage,[[142]](#cite_note-143) which causes frequent blackouts and very high prices. Despite a widening merchandise [trade deficit](/wiki/Balance_of_trade "Balance of trade"), tourism earnings and remittances have helped build [foreign exchange reserves](/wiki/Foreign_exchange_reserves "Foreign exchange reserves"). Following economic turmoil in the late 1980s and 1990, during which the gross domestic product (GDP) fell by up to 5% and consumer price inflation reached an unprecedented 100%, the Dominican Republic entered a period of growth and declining inflation until 2002, after which the economy entered a [recession](/wiki/Recession "Recession").[[110]](#cite_note-ussdnote-111)

This recession followed the collapse of the second-largest [commercial bank](/wiki/Commercial_bank "Commercial bank") in the country, [Baninter](/wiki/Banco_Intercontinental "Banco Intercontinental"), linked to a major incident of fraud valued at US$3.5 billion. The Baninter fraud had a devastating effect on the Dominican economy, with GDP dropping by 1% in 2003 as inflation ballooned by over 27%. All defendants, including the star of the trial, [Ramón Báez Figueroa](/wiki/Ram%C3%B3n_B%C3%A1ez_Figueroa "Ramón Báez Figueroa") (the great-grandson of President [Buenaventura Báez](/wiki/Buenaventura_B%C3%A1ez "Buenaventura Báez")),[[143]](#cite_note-NYTimes_TonySmith-144) were convicted.

According to the 2005 Annual Report of the United Nations Subcommittee on Human Development in the Dominican Republic, the country is ranked No. 71 in the world for resource availability, No. 79 for human development, and No. 14 in the world for resource mismanagement. These statistics emphasize national government corruption, foreign economic interference in the country, and the rift between the rich and poor.

The Dominican Republic has a noted problem of [child labor](/wiki/Child_labor "Child labor") in its coffee, rice, sugarcane, and tomato industries.[[144]](#cite_note-145) The labor injustices in the sugarcane industry extend to forced labor according to the [U.S. Department of Labor](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Labor "United States Department of Labor"). Three large groups own 75% of the land: the State Sugar Council (Consejo Estatal del Azúcar, CEA), [Grupo Vicini](/wiki/Vicini "Vicini"), and Central Romana Corporation.[[145]](#cite_note-Country_Studies-Library_Congress-146)

According to the 2016 [Global Slavery Index](/wiki/Global_Slavery_Index "Global Slavery Index"), an estimated 104,800 people are enslaved in the modern day Dominican Republic, or 1% of the population.[[146]](#cite_note-147)[[147]](#cite_note-148)[[148]](#cite_note-149)

### Currency

Main article: [Dominican peso](/wiki/Dominican_peso "Dominican peso")

The Dominican peso (abbreviated $ or RD$; [ISO 4217](/wiki/ISO_4217 "ISO 4217") code is "DOP")[[149]](#cite_note-XE-150) is the national currency, with the [United States dollar](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar"), the [Euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro"), the [Canadian dollar](/wiki/Canadian_dollar "Canadian dollar") and the [Swiss franc](/wiki/Swiss_franc "Swiss franc") also accepted at most tourist sites. The exchange rate to the U.S. dollar, liberalized by 1985, stood at 2.70 pesos per dollar in August 1986,[[55]](#cite_note-pons-56): p417, 428 14.00 pesos in 1993, and 16.00 pesos in 2000. As of September 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dominican_Republic&action=edit) the rate was 50.08 pesos per dollar.[[150]](#cite_note-151)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Tourism_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Tourism in the Dominican Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Punta_Cana_Beach_Cabeza_De_Toro_1.jpg/250px-Punta_Cana_Beach_Cabeza_De_Toro_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Punta_Cana_Beach_Cabeza_De_Toro_1.jpg)

Cabeza de Toro beach, [Punta Cana](/wiki/Punta_Cana "Punta Cana")

Tourism is one of the fueling factors in the Dominican Republic's economic growth. The Dominican Republic is the most popular tourist destination in the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"). With the construction of projects like [Cap Cana](/wiki/Cap_Cana "Cap Cana"), San Souci Port in Santo Domingo, [Casa De Campo](/wiki/Casa_de_Campo,_Dominican_Republic "Casa de Campo, Dominican Republic") and the Hard Rock Hotel & Casino (ancient Moon Palace Resort) in [Punta Cana](/wiki/Punta_Cana "Punta Cana"), the Dominican Republic expects increased tourism activity in the upcoming years.

[Ecotourism](/wiki/Ecotourism "Ecotourism") has also been a topic increasingly important, with towns like [Jarabacoa](/wiki/Jarabacoa "Jarabacoa") and neighboring [Constanza](/wiki/Constanza,_Dominican_Republic "Constanza, Dominican Republic"), and locations like the [Pico Duarte](/wiki/Pico_Duarte "Pico Duarte"), [Bahía de las Águilas](/wiki/Bah%C3%ADa_de_las_%C3%81guilas "Bahía de las Águilas"), and others becoming more significant in efforts to increase direct benefits from tourism. Most residents from other countries [are required to get a tourist card](/wiki/Visa_policy_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Visa policy of the Dominican Republic"), depending on the country they live in. In the last 10 years the Dominican Republic has become one of the world's notably progressive states in terms of recycling and waste disposal.

### Communications

Main article: [Telecommunications in the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Telecommunications in the Dominican Republic")

The Dominican Republic has a well developed [telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Telecommunications in the Dominican Republic") infrastructure, with extensive mobile phone and [landline](/wiki/Landline "Landline") services. [Cable Internet](/wiki/Cable_Internet_access "Cable Internet access") and [DSL](/wiki/Digital_subscriber_line "Digital subscriber line") are available in most parts of the country, and many [Internet service providers](/wiki/Internet_service_provider "Internet service provider") offer [3G](/wiki/3G "3G") wireless internet service. Projects to extend [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi") hot spots have been made in Santo Domingo.

The telecommunications regulator in the country is INDOTEL (*Instituto Dominicano de Telecomunicaciones*). The largest telecommunications company is [Claro](/wiki/Claro_Rep%C3%BAblica_Dominicana "Claro República Dominicana") – part of [Carlos Slim](/wiki/Carlos_Slim "Carlos Slim")'s [América Móvil](/wiki/Am%C3%A9rica_M%C3%B3vil "América Móvil") – which provides wireless, landline, broadband, and [IPTV](/wiki/IPTV "IPTV") services. In June 2009 there were more than 8 million phone line subscribers (land and cell users) in the D.R., representing 81% of the country's population and a fivefold increase since the year 2000, when there were 1.6 million. The communications sector generates about 3.0% of the GDP.[[151]](#cite_note-LD2009-06-05-152) There were 2,439,997 Internet users in March 2009.[[152]](#cite_note-153)
In November 2009, the Dominican Republic became the first Latin American country to pledge to include a "gender perspective" in every information and communications technology (ICT) initiative and policy developed by the government.[[153]](#cite_note-154) This is part of the regional [eLAC2010](/wiki/ELAC_Action_Plans "ELAC Action Plans") plan. The tool the Dominicans have chosen to design and evaluate all the public policies is the APC [Gender Evaluation Methodology (GEM)](/wiki/Gender_Evaluation_Methodology_(GEM) "Gender Evaluation Methodology (GEM)").

### Electricity

Main article: [Electricity sector in the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Electricity_sector_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Electricity sector in the Dominican Republic")

Electric power service has been unreliable since the Trujillo era, and as much as 75% of the equipment is that old. The country's antiquated power grid causes transmission losses that account for a large share of billed electricity from generators. The privatization of the sector started under a previous administration of Leonel Fernández.[[109]](#cite_note-coha-110) The recent investment in a 345 kilovolt "Santo Domingo–Santiago Electrical Highway"[[154]](#cite_note-DT2009-04-29-155) with reduced [transmission losses](/wiki/Electric_power_transmission#Losses "Electric power transmission"), is being heralded as a major capital improvement to the national grid since the mid-1960s.

During the Trujillo regime electrical service was introduced to many cities. Almost 95% of usage was not billed at all. Around half of the Dominican Republic's 2.1 million houses have no meters and most do not pay or pay a fixed monthly rate for their electric service.[[155]](#cite_note-DT2006-06-01-156)

Household and general electrical service is delivered at 110 [volts](/wiki/Volt "Volt") alternating at 60 [Hz](/wiki/Hertz "Hertz"). Electrically powered items from the United States work with no modifications. The majority of the Dominican Republic has access to electricity. Tourist areas tend to have more reliable power, as do business, travel, healthcare, and vital infrastructure.[[156]](#cite_note-157) Concentrated efforts were announced to increase efficiency of delivery to places where the collection rate reached 70%.[[157]](#cite_note-LD2007-04-11-158) The electricity sector is highly politicized. Some generating companies are undercapitalized and at times unable to purchase adequate fuel supplies.[[110]](#cite_note-ussdnote-111)

### Transportation

Main article: [Transportation in the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Transportation_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Transportation in the Dominican Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Gualey_Santo_Domingo_Dominican_Republic.jpg/250px-Gualey_Santo_Domingo_Dominican_Republic.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gualey_Santo_Domingo_Dominican_Republic.jpg)

[Teleférico de Santo Domingo](/wiki/Telef%C3%A9rico_de_Santo_Domingo "Teleférico de Santo Domingo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/27_de_febrero_av._Santo_Domingo.jpg/250px-27_de_febrero_av._Santo_Domingo.jpg)](/wiki/File:27_de_febrero_av._Santo_Domingo.jpg)

27 de Febrero Avenue in [Santo Domingo](/wiki/Santo_Domingo "Santo Domingo")

The country has three national trunk highways, which connect every major town. These are [DR-1](/wiki/DR-1 "DR-1"), [DR-2](/wiki/DR-2 "DR-2"), and [DR-3](/wiki/DR-3 "DR-3"), which depart from Santo Domingo toward the northern (Cibao), southwestern (Sur), and eastern (El Este) parts of the country respectively. These highways have been consistently improved with the expansion and reconstruction of many sections. Two other national highways serve as spur ([DR-5](/wiki/DR-5 "DR-5")) or alternative routes ([DR-4](/wiki/DR-4 "DR-4")).

In addition to the national highways, the government has embarked on an expansive reconstruction of spur secondary routes, which connect smaller towns to the trunk routes. In the last few years the government constructed a 106-kilometer toll road that connects Santo Domingo with the country's northeastern peninsula. Travelers may now arrive in the Samaná Peninsula in less than two hours. Other additions are the reconstruction of the DR-28 (Jarabacoa – Constanza) and [DR-12](/wiki/DR-12 "DR-12") (Constanza – Bonao). Despite these efforts, many secondary routes still remain either unpaved or in need of maintenance. There is currently a nationwide program to pave these and other commonly used routes. Also, the [Santiago light rail](/wiki/Santiago_light_rail "Santiago light rail") system is in planning stages but currently on hold.

#### Bus

There are two main bus transportation services in the Dominican Republic: one controlled by the government, through the Oficina Técnica de Transito Terrestre (OTTT) and the Oficina Metropolitana de Servicios de Autobuses (OMSA), and the other controlled by private business, among them, Federación Nacional de Transporte La Nueva Opción (FENATRANO) and the Confederacion Nacional de Transporte (CONATRA). The government transportation system covers large routes in metropolitan areas such as Santo Domingo and Santiago.

There are many privately owned bus companies, such as Metro Servicios Turísticos and Caribe Tours, that run daily routes.

#### Santo Domingo Metro

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Stodgo_metro.jpg/250px-Stodgo_metro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stodgo_metro.jpg)

A pair of 9000 series are tested on the Santo Domingo Metro.

Main article: [Santo Domingo Metro](/wiki/Santo_Domingo_Metro "Santo Domingo Metro")

The Dominican Republic has a [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system in [Santo Domingo](/wiki/Santo_Domingo "Santo Domingo"), the country's capital. It is the most extensive metro system in the insular [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") and Central American region by length and number of stations. The Santo Domingo Metro is part of a major "National Master Plan" to improve transportation in Santo Domingo as well as the rest of the nation. The first line was planned to relieve traffic congestion in the [Máximo Gómez](/wiki/DR-1 "DR-1") and [Hermanas Mirabal Avenue](/wiki/DR-13 "DR-13"). The second line, which opened in April 2013, is meant to relieve the congestion along the [Duarte-Kennedy-Centenario](/wiki/DR-13 "DR-13") Corridor in the city from west to east. The current length of the Metro, with the sections of the two lines open as of August 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dominican_Republic&action=edit), is 27.35 kilometres (16.99 mi). Before the opening of the second line, 30,856,515 passengers rode the Santo Domingo Metro in 2012.[[158]](#cite_note-SD-stats-159) With both lines opened, ridership increased to 61,270,054 passengers in 2014.

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Demographics of the Dominican Republic")

The Dominican Republic's population was 11,117,873 in 2021,[[159]](#cite_note-UN_WPP-160)[[160]](#cite_note-UN_WPP_2022-161) compared to 2,380,000 in 1950.[[161]](#cite_note-162) In 2010, 31.2% of the population was under 15 years of age, with 6% of the population over 65 years of age.[[162]](#cite_note-WPP_2012-163) There were an estimated 102.3 males for every 100 females in 2020.[[4]](#cite_note-CIADemo-4) The annual population growth rate for 2006–2007 was 1.5%, with the projected population for the year 2015 being 10,121,000.[[163]](#cite_note-esa-164)

The population density in 2007 was 192 per km2 (498 per sq mi), and 63% of the population lived in urban areas.[[164]](#cite_note-165) The southern coastal plains and the Cibao Valley are the most densely populated areas of the country. The capital city Santo Domingo had a population of 2,907,100 in 2010.

Other important cities are [Santiago de los Caballeros](/wiki/Santiago_de_los_Caballeros "Santiago de los Caballeros") (pop. 745,293), [La Romana](/wiki/La_Romana,_Dominican_Republic "La Romana, Dominican Republic") (pop. 214,109), [San Pedro de Macorís](/wiki/San_Pedro_de_Macor%C3%ADs "San Pedro de Macorís") (pop. 185,255), [Higüey](/wiki/Hig%C3%BCey "Higüey") (153,174), [San Francisco de Macorís](/wiki/San_Francisco_de_Macor%C3%ADs "San Francisco de Macorís") (pop. 132,725), [Puerto Plata](/wiki/Puerto_Plata "Puerto Plata") (pop. 118,282), and [La Vega](/wiki/La_Vega,_Dominican_Republic "La Vega, Dominican Republic") (pop. 104,536). Per the United Nations, the urban population growth rate for 2000–2005 was 2.3%.

### Ethnic groups

Main article: [People of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/People_of_the_Dominican_Republic "People of the Dominican Republic")

Racial groups in the Dominican Republic ([2022 census](/wiki/2022_Dominican_Republic_census "2022 Dominican Republic census"))[[1]](#cite_note-one.gob.do-1)

1. [Mixed](/wiki/Mixed-race_Dominicans "Mixed-race Dominicans") (71.7%)
2. [Whites](/wiki/White_Dominicans "White Dominicans") (18.7%)
3. [Blacks](/wiki/Black_Dominicans "Black Dominicans") (7.45%)
4. [East Asians](/wiki/East_Asian_people "East Asian people") (0.33%)
5. Others (1.80%)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Dominican-people-cibao-1.jpg/250px-Dominican-people-cibao-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dominican-people-cibao-1.jpg)

Dominican Republic people in the town of [Moca](/wiki/Moca,_Espaillat "Moca, Espaillat").

In a 2022 population survey, 71.7% self-identified as [Mixed](/wiki/Mixed-race_Dominicans "Mixed-race Dominicans") (Indio[[b]](#cite_note-170) 34.2%, Moreno 26.1%, [Mestizo](/wiki/Mestizo "Mestizo") 7.7%, [Mulatto](/wiki/Mulatto "Mulatto") 3.8%), 18.7% as [White](/wiki/White_Dominicans "White Dominicans"), 7.4% as [Black](/wiki/Afro-Dominican_(Dominican_Republic) "Afro-Dominican (Dominican Republic)"), and 0.3% as "Other".[[166]](#cite_note-171) According to recent [genealogical DNA studies](/wiki/Genealogical_DNA_test "Genealogical DNA test") of the Dominican population, the genetic makeup is predominantly [European](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Europe "Ethnic groups in Europe") and [Sub-Saharan African](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_African "Sub-Saharan African"), with a lesser degree of [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") ancestry.[[167]](#cite_note-Supplementary_Data-172) The average Dominican DNA of the founder population is estimated to be 73% European, 10% Native, and 17% African. After the Haitian and Afro-Caribbean migrations the overall percentage changed to 57% European, 8% Native and 35% African.[[168]](#cite_note-173) Due to mixed race Dominicans (and most Dominicans in general) being a mix of mainly European and African, with lesser amounts of Indigenous ancestry, they could be described as *"Mulatto"* or *"Tri-racial"*.[[169]](#cite_note-thedominicans.org-174)[[170]](#cite_note-175) Dominican Republic have several informal terms to loosely describe a person's degree of racial admixture, Mestizo means any type of mixed ancestry unlike in other Latin American countries it describes specifically a European/native mix,[[171]](#cite_note-176) *Indio* describes mixed race people whose skin color is between white and black.[[172]](#cite_note-177)

The majority of the Dominican population is tri-racial, with nearly all mixed race individuals having [Taíno](/wiki/Ta%C3%ADno "Taíno") [Native American](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") ancestry along with [European](/wiki/Demographics_of_Europe "Demographics of Europe") (mainly Spanish) and [African](/wiki/Demographics_of_Africa "Demographics of Africa") ancestry. European ancestry in the mixed population typically ranges between 50% and 60% on average, while African ancestry ranges between 30% and 35%, and the Native ancestry usually ranges between 8% and 12%. European and Native ancestry tends to be strongest in cities and towns of the north-central [Cibao](/wiki/Cibao "Cibao") region (60%-80% European, 12-20% Taino), and generally in the mountainous interior of the country. African ancestry is strongest in coastal areas, the southeast plain, and the border regions.[[167]](#cite_note-Supplementary_Data-172) Race in Dominican Republic acts as a continuum of white—mulatto—black due to the large amounts of interracial mixing for hundreds of years in Dominican Republic and the Spanish Caribbean in general, allowing for high amounts of genetic diversity.[[173]](#cite_note-178)[[174]](#cite_note-179)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Dominican_Republic_people%2C_San_Francisco_de_Macoris_music_culture.jpg/250px-Dominican_Republic_people%2C_San_Francisco_de_Macoris_music_culture.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dominican_Republic_people,_San_Francisco_de_Macoris_music_culture.jpg)

Dominican Republic people in Duarte province

Dominican Republic's citizenship is given by *right of blood* ([Jus sanguinis](/wiki/Jus_sanguinis "Jus sanguinis")), not *right of soil*, meaning being born in Dominican Republic does not guarantee citizenship if parents are illegal immigrants.[[175]](#cite_note-180) One would either have to be born in Dominican Republic to parents who are legal citizens or apply for citizenship; citizenship is granted quite easily to people born abroad if they can prove Dominican ancestry.[[176]](#cite_note-181) This means that being a *Dominican citizen* and being an *ethnic Dominican* is not always interchangeable, as the former implies citizenship that one can receive moving from any country in the world to Dominican Republic, while the latter implies a people tied by ancestry and culture. Ethnic Dominicans are people who are not only born in Dominican Republic (and have legal status) or born abroad with ancestral roots in the country, but more importantly have family roots in the country going back several generations and descend from a mix of varying degrees of Spanish, Taino, and African, the three principal foundational roots of Dominican Republic.[[169]](#cite_note-thedominicans.org-174)[[177]](#cite_note-182) Nearly all Dominicans are mixed race, with 75% being "visibly" and "evenly" [mixed](/wiki/Mixed_Dominicans "Mixed Dominicans"), and the remaining 25% being predominantly of African or European blood.[[178]](#cite_note-183) According to a 2017 estimate from the Dominican government, Dominican Republic had a population of 10,189,895, of which 847,979 were immigrants or descendants of recent immigrants and 9,341,916 were *ethnic Dominicans*.[[179]](#cite_note-ENI-2017-184) Most Dominicans embrace all sides of their mixed race heritage, but often identify with their nationality first and foremost. Many Dominicans born in the United States now reside in the Dominican Republic, estimated at around 250,000, creating a kind of expatriate community, who have a growing influence and role in the economic growth of the republic.[[180]](#cite_note-185)[[181]](#cite_note-186)[[182]](#cite_note-187)[[183]](#cite_note-188)

[Haitians](/wiki/Haitians "Haitians") make up the largest ethnic immigrant group in the country, a large majority of them are illegal, in a distant second place are the [Venezuelans](/wiki/Venezuelans "Venezuelans").[[184]](#cite_note-189)[[185]](#cite_note-190) Other groups in the country include the descendants of [West Asians](/wiki/Western_Asia "Western Asia")—mostly [Lebanese](/wiki/Lebanese_people "Lebanese people"), [Syrians](/wiki/Demographics_of_Syria "Demographics of Syria") and [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinian_people "Palestinian people"). A smaller, yet significant presence of [East Asians](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") (primarily [ethnic Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") and [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_people "Japanese people")) can also be found throughout the population. Dominicans are also composed of [Sephardic Jews](/wiki/Sephardic_Jews "Sephardic Jews") that were exiled from [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") and the Mediterranean area in 1492 and 1497,[[186]](#cite_note-191) coupled with other migrations dating to the 1700s[[187]](#cite_note-192) and during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War")[[188]](#cite_note-193) contribute to Dominican ancestry.[[189]](#cite_note-194)[[190]](#cite_note-195)

### Languages

Main article: [Dominican Spanish](/wiki/Dominican_Spanish "Dominican Spanish")

The population of the Dominican Republic is mostly [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language")-speaking, with the only people who do not speak Spanish fluently being some immigrants. The local [variant](/wiki/Variation_(linguistics) "Variation (linguistics)") of Spanish is called [Dominican Spanish](/wiki/Dominican_Spanish "Dominican Spanish"), which closely resembles other Spanish [vernaculars](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") in the Caribbean and has similarities to [Canarian Spanish](/wiki/Canarian_Spanish "Canarian Spanish"). In addition, it has influences from African languages and borrowed words from [indigenous Caribbean languages](/wiki/Indigenous_languages_of_the_Americas#South_America_and_the_Caribbean "Indigenous languages of the Americas") particular to the island of Hispaniola.[[191]](#cite_note-196)[[192]](#cite_note-197) Schools are based on a Spanish educational model; English and French are mandatory foreign languages in both private and public schools,[[193]](#cite_note-198)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] although the quality of foreign languages teaching is poor.[[194]](#cite_note-199)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

[Haitian Creole](/wiki/Haitian_Creole "Haitian Creole") is the largest minority language in the Dominican Republic and is spoken by [Haitian](/wiki/Haitians "Haitians") immigrants and their descendants.[[195]](#cite_note-200) There is a community of a few thousand people whose ancestors spoke [Samaná English](/wiki/Saman%C3%A1_English "Samaná English") in the [Samaná Peninsula](/wiki/Saman%C3%A1_Peninsula "Samaná Peninsula"). They are the descendants of formerly enslaved African Americans who arrived in the nineteenth century, but only a few elders speak the language today.[[196]](#cite_note-201) Tourism, American pop culture, the influence of [Dominican Americans](/wiki/Dominican_Americans "Dominican Americans"), and the country's economic ties with the United States motivate other Dominicans to learn English. The Dominican Republic is ranked 2nd in Latin America and 23rd in the World on English [proficiency](/wiki/Language_proficiency "Language proficiency") as a second language.[[197]](#cite_note-202)[[198]](#cite_note-203)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Religion_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Religion in the Dominican Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Bas%C3%ADlica_Menor_de_Santa_Mar%C3%ADa_SD_RD_02_2017_1941.jpg/250px-Bas%C3%ADlica_Menor_de_Santa_Mar%C3%ADa_SD_RD_02_2017_1941.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bas%C3%ADlica_Menor_de_Santa_Mar%C3%ADa_SD_RD_02_2017_1941.jpg)

The Gothic [Cathedral of Santa María la Menor](/wiki/Basilica_Cathedral_of_Santa_Mar%C3%ADa_la_Menor "Basilica Cathedral of Santa María la Menor"), Santo Domingo, is the oldest cathedral in the Americas, built between 1514 and 1541.

**95.0%**  Christians   
**2.6%**  No religion   
**2.2%**  Other religions [[200]](#cite_note-205)

[Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") is the most widely professed religion in the Dominican Republic.[[2]](#cite_note-:usirf-2) Historically, [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") dominated the religious practices of the country, and as the [official religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") of the state it receives financial support from the government.[[2]](#cite_note-:usirf-2) As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dominican_Republic&action=edit), 57% of the population (5.7 million) identified themselves as [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholics "Roman Catholics") and 23% (2.3 million) as [Protestants](/wiki/Protestants "Protestants") (in Latin American countries, Protestants are often called *Evangelicos* because they emphasize personal and public evangelising and many are [Evangelical Protestant](/wiki/Evangelical_Protestant "Evangelical Protestant") or of a [Pentecostal](/wiki/Pentecostal "Pentecostal") group). From 1896 to 1907 missionaries from the [Episcopal](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)"), [Free Methodist](/wiki/Free_Methodist "Free Methodist"), [Seventh-day Adventist](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist "Seventh-day Adventist") and [Moravian](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church") churches began work in the Dominican Republic.[[201]](#cite_note-Religious_Transformations-206)[[202]](#cite_note-207) Three percent of the 10.63 million Dominican Republic population are Seventh-day Adventists.[[203]](#cite_note-208) Recent immigration as well as proselytizing efforts have brought in other religious groups, with the following shares of the population: [Spiritist](/wiki/Kardecist_spiritism "Kardecist spiritism"): 2.2%,[[204]](#cite_note-religiousfreedom-209) [The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints"): 1.3%,[[205]](#cite_note-210) [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"): 0.1%, [Baháʼí](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith"): 0.1%,[[204]](#cite_note-religiousfreedom-209) [Chinese Folk Religion](/wiki/Chinese_Folk_Religion "Chinese Folk Religion"): 0.1%,[[204]](#cite_note-religiousfreedom-209) Islam: 0.02%, Judaism: 0.01%.

The Catholic Church began to lose its strong dominance in the late 19th century. This was due to a lack of funding, priests, and support programs. During the same time, [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") Evangelicalism began to gain wider support "with their emphasis on personal responsibility and family rejuvenation, economic entrepreneurship, and biblical [fundamentalism](/wiki/Fundamentalism "Fundamentalism")".[[206]](#cite_note-211) The Dominican Republic has two Catholic patroness saints: *Nuestra Señora de la Altagracia* (Our Lady Of High Grace) and *Nuestra Señora de las Mercedes* (Our Lady Of Mercy).

The Dominican Republic has historically granted extensive religious freedom. According to the [United States Department of State](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State"), "The constitution specifies that there is no state church and provides for freedom of religion and belief. A concordat with the Vatican designates Catholicism as the official religion and extends special privileges to the Catholic Church not granted to other religious groups. These include the legal recognition of church law, use of public funds to underwrite some church expenses, and complete exoneration from customs duties."[[207]](#cite_note-212) In the 1950s restrictions were placed upon churches by the government of Trujillo. Letters of protest were sent against the mass arrests of government adversaries. Trujillo began a campaign against the Catholic Church and planned to arrest priests and bishops who preached against the government. This campaign ended before it was put into place, with his assassination.

During World War II a group of Jews escaping [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") fled to the Dominican Republic and founded the city of [Sosúa](/wiki/Sos%C3%BAa "Sosúa"). It has remained the center of the Jewish population since.[[208]](#cite_note-213)

### Immigration in the 20th and 21st centuries

Main articles: [Japanese settlement in the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Japanese_settlement_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Japanese settlement in the Dominican Republic"), [Ethnic Chinese in the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Ethnic_Chinese_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Ethnic Chinese in the Dominican Republic"), and [History of the Jews in the Dominican Republic](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_the_Dominican_Republic "History of the Jews in the Dominican Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Colonia_Japonesa.jpg/250px-Colonia_Japonesa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colonia_Japonesa.jpg)

Family of [Japanese Dominicans](/wiki/Japanese_settlement_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Japanese settlement in the Dominican Republic") in the [Constanza](/wiki/Constanza,_Dominican_Republic "Constanza, Dominican Republic") neighbourhood.

In the 20th century, many [Arabs](/wiki/Arab "Arab") (from the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire")),[[209]](#cite_note-Lebanon-migrants-register-214) Japanese, and, to a lesser degree, [Koreans](/wiki/Koreans "Koreans") settled in the country as agricultural laborers and merchants. The Chinese companies found business in telecom, mining, and railroads. The Arab community is estimated at 80,000.[[209]](#cite_note-Lebanon-migrants-register-214)

Immigrant groups in the country include [West Asians](/wiki/Southwest_Asia "Southwest Asia")—mostly [Lebanese](/wiki/Lebanese_people "Lebanese people"), [Syrians](/wiki/Syrian_people "Syrian people"), and [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinian_people "Palestinian people"); the current president, Luis Abinader, is of Lebanese descent.[[210]](#cite_note-Irrizarri-215)[[211]](#cite_note-Levinson-216) East Asians, [Koreans](/wiki/Koreans "Koreans"),[[212]](#cite_note-migration-217) [ethnic Chinese](/wiki/Ethnic_Chinese_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Ethnic Chinese in the Dominican Republic") and [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_settlement_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Japanese settlement in the Dominican Republic"), can also be found.[[211]](#cite_note-Levinson-216) Europeans are represented mostly by [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_people "Spanish people") whites but also with smaller populations of [Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans"),[[212]](#cite_note-migration-217) [Italians](/wiki/Italian_people "Italian people"), [French](/wiki/French_people "French people"), [British](/wiki/British_people "British people"),[[213]](#cite_note-218)[[212]](#cite_note-migration-217) [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_people "Dutch people"), [Swiss](/wiki/Swiss_people "Swiss people"),[[212]](#cite_note-migration-217) [Russians](/wiki/Russians "Russians"),[[212]](#cite_note-migration-217) and [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarian_people "Hungarian people").[[211]](#cite_note-Levinson-216)

In addition, there are descendants of immigrants who came from other Caribbean islands, including [St. Kitts and Nevis](/wiki/St._Kitts_and_Nevis "St. Kitts and Nevis"), [Antigua](/wiki/Antigua "Antigua"), [St. Vincent](/wiki/Saint_Vincent_(Saint_Vincent_and_the_Grenadines) "Saint Vincent (Saint Vincent and the Grenadines)"), [Montserrat](/wiki/Montserrat "Montserrat"), [Tortola](/wiki/Tortola "Tortola"), [St. Croix](/wiki/Saint_Croix,_U.S._Virgin_Islands "Saint Croix, U.S. Virgin Islands"), [St. Thomas](/wiki/St._Thomas,_U.S._Virgin_Islands "St. Thomas, U.S. Virgin Islands"), and [Guadeloupe](/wiki/Guadeloupe "Guadeloupe"). They are known locally as *[Cocolos](/wiki/Cocolo "Cocolo")*. They worked on sugarcane plantations and docks and settled mainly in the cities of [San Pedro de Macorís](/wiki/San_Pedro_de_Macor%C3%ADs "San Pedro de Macorís") and [Puerto Plata](/wiki/Puerto_Plata "Puerto Plata"). Puerto Rican, and to a lesser extent, Cuban immigrants fled to the Dominican Republic from the mid-1800s until about 1940 due to a poor economy and social unrest in their respective home countries.[[214]](#cite_note-219) Many Puerto Rican immigrants settled in [Higüey](/wiki/Hig%C3%BCey_(city) "Higüey (city)"), among other cities, and quickly assimilated due to similar culture. Before and during World War II, 800 Jewish refugees moved to the Dominican Republic.[[215]](#cite_note-SosuaJewishStudies-220)

Numerous immigrants have come from other Caribbean countries, as the country has offered economic opportunities. There are many Haitians and [Venezuelans](/wiki/Venezuelans "Venezuelans") living in the Dominican Republic. They are the largest immigrant groups in the country currently, and large numbers of both groups are present in the country illegally.[[4]](#cite_note-CIADemo-4) There is an increasing number of well-off [Puerto Rican](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_people "Puerto Rican people") immigrants, owning businesses and vacation homes in the country, many retiring there, they are believed to number around 10,000.[[216]](#cite_note-221)[[217]](#cite_note-222) Many Europeans and Americans (non-Puerto Rican) are also retiring in the country.[[218]](#cite_note-223) About 300,000 U.S. citizens reside in Dominican Republic, of which 250,000 are [Dominican Americans](/wiki/Dominican_Americans "Dominican Americans") whom have returned to the country, and 50,000 are Americans of non-Dominican ancestry from the mainland United States and the U.S. territory of Puerto Rico.[[219]](#cite_note-224)[[220]](#cite_note-225)

The [2010 Census](/wiki/2010_Dominican_Republic_Census "2010 Dominican Republic Census") registered 311,969 Haitians; 24,457 Americans; 6,691 Spaniards; 5,763 Puerto Ricans; and 5,132 Venezuelans.[[221]](#cite_note-226) In 2012, the Dominican government made a survey of immigrants in the country and found that there were: 329,281 [Haitian-born](/wiki/Haitians_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Haitians in the Dominican Republic"); 25,814 [U.S.](/wiki/Americans "Americans")-born (excluding Puerto Rican-born); 7,062 [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_people "Spanish people")-born; 6,083 [Puerto Rican](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_people "Puerto Rican people")-born; 5,417 [Venezuelan](/wiki/Venezuelan_people "Venezuelan people")-born; 3,841 [Cuban](/wiki/Cubans "Cubans")-born; 3,795 [Italian](/wiki/Italians "Italians")-born; 3,606 [Colombian](/wiki/Colombian_people "Colombian people")-born; 2,043 [French](/wiki/French_people "French people")-born; 1,661 [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans")-born; 1,484 [Chinese-born](/wiki/Ethnic_Chinese_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Ethnic Chinese in the Dominican Republic"); among others.[[222]](#cite_note-227)[[223]](#cite_note-228)[[224]](#cite_note-ONE-229)[[225]](#cite_note-230) In the second half of 2017, a second survey of foreign population was conducted in the Dominican Republic. The total population in the Dominican Republic was estimated at 10,189,895, of which 9,341,916 were Dominicans with no foreign background. According to the survey, the majority of the people with foreign background were of Haitian origin (751,080 out of 847,979, or 88.6%), breaking down as follows: 497,825 were Haitians born in Haiti, 171,859 Haitians born in the Dominican Republic and 81,590 Dominicans with a Haitian parent. Other main sources of foreign-born population were Venezuela (25,872), the United States (10,016), Spain (7,592), Italy (3,713), [China](/wiki/Two_Chinas "Two Chinas") (3,069), Colombia (2,642), Puerto Rico (2,356), and Cuba (2,024).[[179]](#cite_note-ENI-2017-184)

#### Haitian immigration

Main article: [Haitians in the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Haitians_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Haitians in the Dominican Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Haiti_deforestation.jpg/250px-Haiti_deforestation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Haiti_deforestation.jpg)

A satellite image of the [border](/wiki/Dominican_Republic%E2%80%93Haiti_border "Dominican Republic–Haiti border") between [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti") (left) and the Dominican Republic (right), highlighting the [deforestation on the Haitian side](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Haiti "Deforestation in Haiti")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Dominicans_and_Haitians_Braving_the_Weather.jpg/250px-Dominicans_and_Haitians_Braving_the_Weather.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dominicans_and_Haitians_Braving_the_Weather.jpg)

Dominicans and Haitians lined up to attend medical providers from the [U.S. Army Reserve](/wiki/U.S._Army_Reserve "U.S. Army Reserve")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Haitian-Dominican_border_in_Grand-Bois%2C_Cornillon%2C_Haiti%2C_June_2017_3.jpg/250px-Haitian-Dominican_border_in_Grand-Bois%2C_Cornillon%2C_Haiti%2C_June_2017_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Haitian-Dominican_border_in_Grand-Bois,_Cornillon,_Haiti,_June_2017_3.jpg)

View of border region between the Dominican Republic and Haiti. The border runs horizontally through the middle of the picture.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/One_Too_Many%3B_50%2B_Haitian_Workers_In_Transit.jpg/250px-One_Too_Many%3B_50%2B_Haitian_Workers_In_Transit.jpg)](/wiki/File:One_Too_Many;_50%2B_Haitian_Workers_In_Transit.jpg)

Haitian workers being transported in [Punta Cana](/wiki/Punta_Cana "Punta Cana"), the Dominican Republic

[Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch") estimated that 70,000 documented Haitian immigrants and 1,930,000 undocumented immigrants were living in Dominican Republic.[[c]](#cite_note-236)

Haiti is the neighboring nation to the Dominican Republic and is considerably poorer, less developed and is additionally the least developed country in the western hemisphere. In 2003, 80% of all Haitians were poor (54% living in abject poverty) and 47.1% were illiterate. The country of nine million people also has a fast growing population, but over two-thirds of the labor force lack formal jobs. Haiti's per capita GDP (PPP) was $1,800 in 2017, or just over one-tenth of the Dominican figure.[[4]](#cite_note-CIADemo-4)[[231]](#cite_note-237)

As a result, hundreds of thousands of Haitians have migrated to the Dominican Republic, with some estimates of 800,000 Haitians in the country,[[140]](#cite_note-pinadep-141) while others put the Haitian-born population as high as one million.[[232]](#cite_note-238) They usually work at low-paying and unskilled jobs in building construction and house cleaning and in sugar plantations.[[233]](#cite_note-ferguson-239) There have been accusations that some Haitian immigrants work in slavery-like conditions and are severely exploited.[[234]](#cite_note-240)

Due to the lack of basic amenities and medical facilities in Haiti a large number of Haitian women, often arriving with several health problems, cross the border to Dominican soil. They deliberately come during their last weeks of pregnancy to obtain medical attention for childbirth, since Dominican public hospitals do not refuse medical services based on nationality or legal status. Statistics from a hospital in Santo Domingo report that over 22% of childbirths are by Haitian mothers.[[235]](#cite_note-LD2008-01-21-241)

Haiti also [suffers from severe environmental degradation](/wiki/Environmental_issues_in_Haiti "Environmental issues in Haiti"). Deforestation is rampant in Haiti; today less than 4 percent of Haiti's forests remain, and in many places the soil has eroded right down to the bedrock.[[236]](#cite_note-242) Haitians burn wood charcoal for 60% of their domestic energy production. Because of Haiti running out of plant material to burn, some Haitian bootleggers have created an illegal market for charcoal on the Dominican side. Conservative estimates calculate the illegal movement of 115 tons of charcoal per week from the Dominican Republic to Haiti. Dominican officials estimate that at least 10 trucks per week are crossing the border loaded with charcoal.[[237]](#cite_note-243)

In 2005, Dominican President Leonel Fernández criticized collective expulsions of Haitians as having taken place "in an abusive and inhuman way".[[238]](#cite_note-244) After a UN delegation issued a preliminary report stating that it found a profound problem of racism and discrimination against people of Haitian origin, Dominican [Foreign Minister](/wiki/Foreign_Minister "Foreign Minister") [Carlos Morales Troncoso](/wiki/Carlos_Morales_Troncoso "Carlos Morales Troncoso") issued a formal statement denouncing it, asserting that "our border with Haiti has its problems[;] this is our reality and it must be understood. It is important not to confuse national sovereignty with indifference, and not to confuse security with [xenophobia](/wiki/Xenophobia "Xenophobia")."[[239]](#cite_note-245)

Haitian nationals send half a [billion](/wiki/Billion "Billion") [dollars](/wiki/Dollars "Dollars") total yearly in remittance from the Dominican Republic to Haiti, according to the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank").[[240]](#cite_note-246)

The government of the Dominican Republic invested a total of $16 billion pesos in health services offered to foreign patients in 2013–2016, according to official data, which includes medical expenses in blood transfusion, clinical analysis, surgeries and other care.[[241]](#cite_note-247) According to official reports, the country spends more than five billion Dominican pesos annually in care for pregnant women who cross the border ready to deliver.[[242]](#cite_note-248)

The children of Haitian immigrants are eligible for Haitian nationality,[[243]](#cite_note-249) but they may be denied it by Haiti because of a lack of proper documents or witnesses.[[244]](#cite_note-250)[[245]](#cite_note-251)[[246]](#cite_note-252)[[247]](#cite_note-253)

In 2023, the Dominican Republic deported over 185,000 Haitian men, women, and children, followed by more than 230,000 in 2024 and a record 370,000 in 2025, many of whom were deported in caged trucks.[[248]](#cite_note-254)[[249]](#cite_note-255)[[250]](#cite_note-256) The Dominican Republic ignored the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations")' call to halt [deportations](/wiki/Deportation "Deportation") to Haiti, where over 5.7 million people faced [famine in 2024](/wiki/2024_famine_in_Haiti "2024 famine in Haiti").[[251]](#cite_note-257)

### Emigration

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Dominican_Day_Parade_2019_%2850335870922%29.jpg/250px-Dominican_Day_Parade_2019_%2850335870922%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dominican_Day_Parade_2019_(50335870922).jpg)

Dominicans in the [Dominican Day Parade](/wiki/Dominican_Day_Parade "Dominican Day Parade") in New York City, 2019

Main articles: [Dominican Americans](/wiki/Dominican_Americans "Dominican Americans") and [Dominicans in Spain](/wiki/Dominicans_in_Spain "Dominicans in Spain")

The first of three late-20th century emigration waves began in 1961 after the assassination of dictator Trujillo,[[252]](#cite_note-Wilderotter-258) due to fear of retaliation by Trujillo's allies and political uncertainty in general. In 1965, the United States began a military occupation of the Dominican Republic to end a civil war. Upon this, the U.S. eased travel restrictions, making it easier for Dominicans to obtain U.S. visas.[[253]](#cite_note-259) From 1966 to 1978, the exodus continued, fueled by high unemployment and political repression. Communities established by the first wave of immigrants to the U.S. created a network that assisted subsequent arrivals.[[254]](#cite_note-Annenberg_Foundation-260)

In the early 1980s, underemployment, inflation, and the rise in value of the dollar all contributed to a third wave of emigration from the Dominican Republic. Today, emigration from the Dominican Republic remains high.[[254]](#cite_note-Annenberg_Foundation-260) In 2012, there were approximately 1.7 million people of Dominican descent in the U.S., counting both native- and foreign-born.[[255]](#cite_note-261) There was also a growing [Dominican immigration to Puerto Rico](/wiki/Dominican_Republic_immigration_to_Puerto_Rico "Dominican Republic immigration to Puerto Rico"), with nearly 70,000 Dominicans living there as of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dominican_Republic&action=edit). Although that number is slowly decreasing and immigration trends have reversed because of Puerto Rico's economic crisis as of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dominican_Republic&action=edit).

There is a significant Dominican population in Spain.[[256]](#cite_note-INE2018nacidos-262)[[257]](#cite_note-INE2018nacionalidad-263)

### Education

Main article: [Education in the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Education_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Education in the Dominican Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Dominican_kids_in_Santo_Domingo.jpg/250px-Dominican_kids_in_Santo_Domingo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dominican_kids_in_Santo_Domingo.jpg)

Kids taking classes

Primary education is regulated by the Ministry of Education, with education being a right of all citizens and [youth in the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Youth_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Youth in the Dominican Republic").[[258]](#cite_note-264)

Preschool education is organized in different cycles and serves the 2–4 age group and the 4–6 age group. Preschool education is not mandatory except for the last year. Basic education is compulsory and serves the population of the 6–14 age group. Secondary education is not compulsory, although it is the duty of the state to offer it for free. It caters to the 14–18 age group and is organized in a common core of four years and three modes of two years of study that are offered in three different options: general or academic, vocational (industrial, agricultural, and services), and artistic.

The higher education system consists of institutes and universities. The institutes offer courses of a higher technical level. The universities offer technical careers, undergraduate and graduate; these are regulated by the Ministry of Higher Education, Science and Technology.[[259]](#cite_note-265) The Dominican Republic was ranked 97th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025, down from 87th in 2019.[[260]](#cite_note-266)[[261]](#cite_note-267)[[262]](#cite_note-268)

### Health

Main article: [Health in the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Health_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Health in the Dominican Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/HOMS_182.jpg/250px-HOMS_182.jpg)](/wiki/File:HOMS_182.jpg)

[Hospital Metropolitano de Santiago](/wiki/Hospital_Metropolitano_de_Santiago "Hospital Metropolitano de Santiago") is the largest hospital in the Dominican Republic

In 2020, the Dominican Republic had an estimated [birth rate](/wiki/Birth_rate "Birth rate") of 18.5 per 1000 and a [death rate](/wiki/Death_rate "Death rate") of 6.3 per 1000.[[4]](#cite_note-CIADemo-4)

In the 2024 Global Hunger Index (GHI), the Dominican Republic ranks 41st out of 127 countries with sufficient data. The Dominican Republic's GHI score is 7.8, which indicates a low level of hunger.[[263]](#cite_note-269)

According to the [WHO](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization"), the estimated incidence rate of [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis") (TB) in the Dominican Republic (42 cases per 100,000 people in 2023) is one of the highest in the Caribbean (after Haiti).[[264]](#cite_note-270)

The Dominican Republic has a 0.9% prevalence rate of [HIV/AIDS](/wiki/HIV/AIDS_in_the_Dominican_Republic "HIV/AIDS in the Dominican Republic").[[265]](#cite_note-271) Some urban areas of the Dominican Republic experience HIV/AIDS infection rates well in excess of 10%.[[266]](#cite_note-Patria-272) In some parts of the Dominican Republic, HIV/AIDS has become one of the leading cause of death among teenagers and adults between 15–49 years old.[[266]](#cite_note-Patria-272)

### Crime

Main article: [Crime in the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Crime_in_the_Dominican_Republic "Crime in the Dominican Republic")

In 2012, the Dominican Republic had a [murder rate](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_intentional_homicide_rate "List of countries by intentional homicide rate") of 22.1 per 100,000 population.[[267]](#cite_note-UNODC-273) There was a total of 2,268 murders in the Dominican Republic in 2012.[[267]](#cite_note-UNODC-273)

The Dominican Republic has become a trans-shipment point for Colombian drugs destined for Europe as well as the United States and Canada.[[4]](#cite_note-CIADemo-4)[[268]](#cite_note-NYT-274) Money-laundering via the Dominican Republic is favored by Colombian drug cartels for the ease of illicit financial transactions.[[4]](#cite_note-CIADemo-4) In 2004, it was estimated that 8% of all cocaine smuggled into the United States had come through the Dominican Republic.[[269]](#cite_note-275) The Dominican Republic responded with increased efforts to seize drug shipments, arrest and extradite those involved, and combat money-laundering.

The often-light treatment of violent criminals has been a continuous source of local controversy. In April 2010, five teenagers, aged 15 to 17, shot and killed two taxi drivers and killed another five by forcing them to drink drain-cleaning acid. On September 24, 2010, the teens were sentenced to prison terms of three to five years, despite the protests of the taxi drivers' families.[[270]](#cite_note-276)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Culture_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Culture of the Dominican Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/03/Yoryi_Morel_02.jpg/250px-Yoryi_Morel_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yoryi_Morel_02.jpg)

Campesino cibaeño, 1941 (Museo de Arte Moderno, Santo Domingo)

Due to [cultural syncretism](/wiki/Cultural_syncretism "Cultural syncretism"), the culture and customs of the Dominican people have a [European cultural basis](/wiki/European_culture "European culture"), influenced by both [African](/wiki/African_culture "African culture") and native [Taíno](/wiki/Ta%C3%ADno "Taíno") elements, although endogenous elements have emerged within Dominican culture;[[271]](#cite_note-EstevaFabregat-277) culturally the Dominican Republic is among the most-European countries in [Spanish America](/wiki/Spanish_America "Spanish America"), alongside [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Culture_of_Puerto_Rico "Culture of Puerto Rico"), [Cuba](/wiki/Culture_of_Cuba "Culture of Cuba"), [Central Chile](/wiki/Central_Chile "Central Chile"), [Argentina](/wiki/Culture_of_Argentina "Culture of Argentina"), and [Uruguay](/wiki/Culture_of_Uruguay "Culture of Uruguay").[[271]](#cite_note-EstevaFabregat-277) Spanish institutions in the colonial era were able to predominate in the Dominican culture's making-of as a relative success in the [acculturation](/wiki/Acculturation "Acculturation") and [cultural assimilation](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation "Cultural assimilation") of African slaves slightly diminished African cultural influence in comparison to other Caribbean countries.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### National symbols

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Pereskia_quisqueyana.JPG/250px-Pereskia_quisqueyana.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pereskia_quisqueyana.JPG)

Bayahibe rose

Some of the Dominican Republic's important symbols are the [flag](/wiki/Flag_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Flag of the Dominican Republic"), [the coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Coat of arms of the Dominican Republic"), and the national anthem, titled *[Himno Nacional](/wiki/National_Anthem_of_the_Dominican_Republic "National Anthem of the Dominican Republic")*. The flag has a large white cross that divides it into four quarters. Two quarters are red and two are blue. Red represents the blood shed by the liberators. Blue expresses God's protection over the nation. The white cross symbolizes the struggle of the liberators to bequeath future generations a free nation. An alternative interpretation is that blue represents the ideals of progress and liberty, whereas white symbolizes peace and unity among Dominicans.[[272]](#cite_note-278)

In the center of the cross is the Dominican coat of arms, in the same colors as the national flag. The coat of arms pictures a red, white, and blue flag-draped shield with a Bible, a gold cross, and arrows; the shield is surrounded by an olive branch (on the left) and a palm branch (on the right). The Bible traditionally represents the truth and the light. The gold cross symbolizes the redemption from slavery, and the arrows symbolize the noble soldiers and their proud military. A blue ribbon above the shield reads, "Dios, Patria, Libertad" (meaning "God, Fatherland, Liberty"). A red ribbon under the shield reads, "República Dominicana" (meaning "Dominican Republic"). Out of all the flags in the world, the depiction of a Bible is unique to the Dominican flag.

The [national flower](/wiki/Floral_emblem "Floral emblem") is the [endemic](/wiki/Endemic "Endemic") [Bayahibe rose](/wiki/Pereskia_quisqueyana "Pereskia quisqueyana") (*Leuenbergeria quisqueyana*) and the [national tree](/wiki/National_tree "National tree") is the [West Indian mahogany](/wiki/Swietenia_mahagoni "Swietenia mahagoni") (*Swietenia mahagoni*).[[273]](#cite_note-279) The national bird is the *cigua palmera* or [palmchat](/wiki/Palmchat "Palmchat") (*Dulus dominicus*), another endemic species.[[274]](#cite_note-depaul-280)

The Dominican Republic celebrates [Dia de la Altagracia](/wiki/Dia_de_la_Altagracia "Dia de la Altagracia") on January 21 in honor of its patroness, [Duarte](/wiki/Juan_Pablo_Duarte "Juan Pablo Duarte")'s Day on January 26 in honor of one of its founding fathers, Independence Day on February 27, [Restoration Day](/wiki/Dominican_Restoration_War "Dominican Restoration War") on August 16, *Virgen de las Mercedes* on September 24, and [Constitution Day](/wiki/Constitution_Day "Constitution Day") on November 6.

### Architecture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Church_and_Convent_Regina_Angelorum_CCSD_07_2018_0762.jpg/250px-Church_and_Convent_Regina_Angelorum_CCSD_07_2018_0762.jpg)](/wiki/File:Church_and_Convent_Regina_Angelorum_CCSD_07_2018_0762.jpg)

Church and Convent, Colonial Santo Domingo

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Downtown_Puerto_Plata_Dominican_Republic_Architecture.jpg/250px-Downtown_Puerto_Plata_Dominican_Republic_Architecture.jpg)](/wiki/File:Downtown_Puerto_Plata_Dominican_Republic_Architecture.jpg)

Late 19th-century Victorian architecture is prominent in Puerto Plata

The architecture in the Dominican Republic represents a complex blend of diverse cultures. The deep influence of the [European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") colonists is the most evident throughout the country. Characterized by ornate designs and [baroque](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") structures, the style can best be seen in the capital city of [Santo Domingo](/wiki/Santo_Domingo "Santo Domingo"), which is home to the first cathedral, palace, monastery, and fortress in all of the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), located in the city's [Colonial Zone](/wiki/Ciudad_Colonial_(Santo_Domingo) "Ciudad Colonial (Santo Domingo)"), an area declared as a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO").[[27]](#cite_note-Colonial_City_of_Santo_Domingo-28)[[275]](#cite_note-281) The designs carry over into the villas and buildings throughout the country. It can also be observed on buildings that contain stucco exteriors, arched doors and windows, and red tiled roofs.

The indigenous peoples of the Dominican Republic have also had a significant influence on the architecture of the country. The [Taíno](/wiki/Ta%C3%ADno "Taíno") people relied heavily on the mahogany and guano (dried palm tree leaf) to put together crafts, artwork, furniture, and houses. Utilizing mud, thatched roofs, and mahogany trees, they gave buildings and the furniture inside a natural look, blending in with the island's surroundings.

Lately, with the rise in tourism and increasing popularity as a Caribbean vacation destination, architects in the Dominican Republic have now begun to incorporate cutting-edge designs that emphasize luxury. In many ways an architectural playground, villas and hotels implement new styles, while offering new takes on the old. This new style is characterized by simplified, angular corners and large windows that blend outdoor and indoor spaces.

### Visual arts

Dominican art is perhaps most commonly associated with the bright, vibrant colors and images that are sold in every tourist gift shop across the country. However, the country has a long history of [fine art](/wiki/Fine_art "Fine art") that goes back to the middle of the 1800s when the country became independent and the beginnings of a national art scene emerged.

Historically, the painting of this time were centered around images connected to national independence, historical scenes, portraits but also landscapes and images of still life. Styles of painting ranged between [neoclassicism](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism") and [romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism"). Between 1920 and 1940 the art scene was influenced by styles of [realism](/wiki/Realism_(arts) "Realism (arts)") and [impressionism](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism"). Dominican artists were focused on breaking from previous, academic styles in order to develop more independent and individual styles.

### Literature

Main article: [Dominican Republic literature](/wiki/Dominican_Republic_literature "Dominican Republic literature")

The Dominican politician, rector and author [Andrés López de Medrano](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9s_L%C3%B3pez_de_Medrano "Andrés López de Medrano") (1780 – May 6, 1856) became the first enlightened philosopher of the Dominican Republic and supported Dominican independence.[[276]](#cite_note-:1-282) Medrano is best known for writing one of the most important [philosophical](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") works of the 19th century, a treaty or guide entitled *Logic, Elements of Modern Philosophy* (1814), which became the first book of Dominican philosophy and the first book printed in the Dominican Republic.[[276]](#cite_note-:1-282)

The 20th century brought many prominent Dominican writers, and saw a general increase in the perception of Dominican literature. Writers such as [Juan Bosch](/wiki/Juan_Bosch_(politician) "Juan Bosch (politician)"), [Pedro Mir](/wiki/Pedro_Mir "Pedro Mir") (national poet of the Dominican Republic[[277]](#cite_note-283)[[278]](#cite_note-284)[[279]](#cite_note-285)), [Aida Cartagena Portalatin](/wiki/Aida_Cartagena_Portalatin "Aida Cartagena Portalatin"), Emilio Rodríguez Demorizi (the most important Dominican historian, with more than 1000 written works[[280]](#cite_note-286)[[281]](#cite_note-287)[[282]](#cite_note-288)[[283]](#cite_note-289)), [Manuel del Cabral](/wiki/Manuel_del_Cabral "Manuel del Cabral") (main Dominican poet featured in black poetry[[284]](#cite_note-290)[[285]](#cite_note-291)), Hector Inchustegui Cabral (considered one of the most prominent voices of the Caribbean social poetry of the twentieth century[[286]](#cite_note-292)[[287]](#cite_note-293)[[288]](#cite_note-294)[[289]](#cite_note-295)), Miguel Alfonseca (poet belonging to Generation 60[[290]](#cite_note-296)[[291]](#cite_note-297)), Rene del Risco (acclaimed poet who was a participant in the June 14 Movement[[292]](#cite_note-298)[[293]](#cite_note-299)[[294]](#cite_note-300)), [Mateo Morrison](/wiki/Mateo_Morrison "Mateo Morrison"), among many more prolific authors, put the island in one of the most important in Literature in the twentieth century.

New Dominican writers have not yet achieved the renown of their 20th-century counterparts. However, writers such as [Frank Báez](/wiki/Frank_B%C3%A1ez "Frank Báez") (won the 2006 Santo Domingo Book Fair First Prize) [[295]](#cite_note-301)[[296]](#cite_note-302) and [Junot Díaz](/wiki/Junot_D%C3%ADaz "Junot Díaz") (2008 [Pulitzer Prize for Fiction](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Fiction "Pulitzer Prize for Fiction") for his novel *[The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao](/wiki/The_Brief_Wondrous_Life_of_Oscar_Wao "The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao"))*[[297]](#cite_note-303) lead Dominican literature in the 21st century.

### Music and dance

Main article: [Music of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/Music_of_the_Dominican_Republic "Music of the Dominican Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Romeo_Santos_y_Juan_Luis_Guerra.jpg/250px-Romeo_Santos_y_Juan_Luis_Guerra.jpg)](/wiki/File:Romeo_Santos_y_Juan_Luis_Guerra.jpg)

[Merengue](/wiki/Merengue_music "Merengue music"), sung by [Juan Luis Guerra](/wiki/Juan_Luis_Guerra "Juan Luis Guerra") (left), and [bachata](/wiki/Bachata_(music) "Bachata (music)"), sung by [Romeo Santos](/wiki/Romeo_Santos "Romeo Santos") (right), are two very popular music genres native to the Dominican Republic.

Musically, the Dominican Republic is known for the [world popular](/wiki/Popular_culture "Popular culture") [musical style](/wiki/Musical_style "Musical style") and genre called [merengue](/wiki/Merengue_music "Merengue music"),[[298]](#cite_note-Harvey2006-304): 376–7 a type of lively, fast-paced rhythm and dance music consisting of a tempo of about 120 to 160 beats per minute (though it varies) based on musical elements like drums, brass, chorded instruments, and accordion, as well as some elements unique to the Spanish-speaking Caribbean, such as the *[tambora](/wiki/Tambora_(Dominican_drum) "Tambora (Dominican drum)")* and *[güira](/wiki/G%C3%BCira "Güira")*.

Its [syncopated beats](/wiki/Syncopation "Syncopation") use [Latin percussion](/wiki/Latin_percussion "Latin percussion"), [brass instruments](/wiki/Brass_instruments "Brass instruments"), bass, and piano or keyboard. Between 1937 and 1950 merengue music was promoted internationally by Dominican groups like Billo's Caracas Boys, Chapuseaux and Damiron "Los Reyes del Merengue", Joseito Mateo, and others. Radio, television, and international media popularized it further. Some well known merengue performers are [Wilfrido Vargas](/wiki/Wilfrido_Vargas "Wilfrido Vargas"), [Johnny Ventura](/wiki/Johnny_Ventura "Johnny Ventura"), singer-songwriter [Los Hermanos Rosario](/wiki/Los_Hermanos_Rosario "Los Hermanos Rosario"), [Juan Luis Guerra](/wiki/Juan_Luis_Guerra "Juan Luis Guerra"), [Fernando Villalona](/wiki/Fernando_Villalona "Fernando Villalona"), [Eddy Herrera](/wiki/Eddy_Herrera "Eddy Herrera"), [Sergio Vargas](/wiki/Sergio_Vargas "Sergio Vargas"), [Toño Rosario](/wiki/To%C3%B1o_Rosario "Toño Rosario"), [Milly Quezada](/wiki/Milly_Quezada "Milly Quezada"), and [Chichí Peralta](/wiki/Chich%C3%AD_Peralta "Chichí Peralta").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Dominican_Republic_Dance.jpg/250px-Dominican_Republic_Dance.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dominican_Republic_Dance.jpg)

Dominicans dancing in parade with traditional dress

[Bachata](/wiki/Bachata_(music) "Bachata (music)"), a form of music and dance that originated in the countryside and rural marginal neighborhoods of the Dominican Republic, has become quite popular in recent years. Its subjects are often romantic; especially prevalent are tales of heartbreak and sadness. In fact, the original name for the genre was *amargue* ("bitterness", or "bitter music"), until the rather ambiguous (and mood-neutral) term *bachata* became popular. Bachata grew out of, and is still closely related to, the [pan-Latin](/wiki/Pan-Latinism "Pan-Latinism") American romantic style called *[bolero](/wiki/Bolero "Bolero")*. Over time, it has been influenced by merengue and by a variety of Latin American guitar styles.

[Palo](/wiki/Palo_music "Palo music") is an [Afro-Dominican](/wiki/Afro-Dominican_(Dominican_Republic) "Afro-Dominican (Dominican Republic)") sacred music that can be found throughout the island. The drum and human voice are the principal instruments. Palo is played at religious ceremonies—usually coinciding with saints' religious feast days—as well as for secular parties and special occasions. Its roots are in the [Congo region](/wiki/Congo_region "Congo region") of central-west Africa, but it is mixed with European influences in the melodies.[[299]](#cite_note-305)

[Salsa music](/wiki/Salsa_music "Salsa music") has had a great deal of popularity in the country. During the late 1960s Dominican musicians like [Johnny Pacheco](/wiki/Johnny_Pacheco "Johnny Pacheco"), creator of the [Fania All Stars](/wiki/Fania_All_Stars "Fania All Stars"), played a significant role in the development and popularization of the genre.

[Dominican rock](/wiki/Dominican_rock "Dominican rock") and [Reggaeton](/wiki/Reggaeton "Reggaeton") are also popular. Many, if not the majority, of its performers are based in Santo Domingo and Santiago.

### Fashion

The country boasts one of the ten most important design schools in the region, La Escuela de Diseño de Altos de Chavón, which is making the country a key player in the world of fashion and design. Noted fashion designer [Oscar de la Renta](/wiki/Oscar_de_la_Renta "Oscar de la Renta") was born in the Dominican Republic in 1932, and became a US citizen in 1971. By 1963, he had designs bearing his own label. After establishing himself in the US, de la Renta opened boutiques across the country.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] His work blends French and Spaniard fashion with American styles.[[300]](#cite_note-306)[[301]](#cite_note-britannica_de_la_renta-307) Although he settled in New York, de la Renta also marketed his work in Latin America, where it became very popular, and remained active in his native Dominican Republic, where his charitable activities and personal achievements earned him the Juan Pablo Duarte Order of Merit and the Order of Cristóbal Colón.[[301]](#cite_note-britannica_de_la_renta-307) De la Renta died of complications from cancer on October 20, 2014.

### Cuisine
